Abstract Recent focus on Culicoides species diversity in Thailand was prompted by a need to identify vectors respon - sible for the transmission of African Horse Sickness in that country. To assist rapid genetic identification of spe- cies, we sampled mitochondrial cytochrome c oxidase subunit I (COI) DNA barcodes (N = 78) from 40 species of Culicoides biting midge from Thailand, including 17 species for which DNA barcodes were previously una - vailable. The DNA barcodes were assigned to 39 Barcode Identification Numbers (BINs) representing terminal genetic clusters at the Barcode of Life Data systems (BOLD). BINs assisted with comparisons to published conspecific DNA barcodes and allowed partial barcodes obtained from seven specimens to be associated with BINs by their similarity. Some taxonomic issues were revealed and attributed to the possible misident

ained from seven specimens to be associated with BINs by their similarity. Some taxonomic issues were revealed and attributed to the possible misidentification of earlier reported specimens as well as a potential synonymy of C. elbeli Wirth & Hubert and C. menglaensis Chu & Liu. Comparison with published BINs also revealed genetic evidence of divergent population processes and or potentially cryptic species in 16 described taxa, flagged by their high levels of COI sequence differ- ence among conspecifics. We recommend the BOLD BIN system to researchers preparing DNA barcodes of vouchered species for public release. This will alert them to taxonomic incongruencies between their records and publicly released DNA barcodes, and also flag genetically deep and potentially novel diversity in described species. Keywords: biting midge, Barcode Index Number, cryptic species, vector Biting midges of the genus Culicoides Latreille (Diptera: Ceratopogonidae) are small, blood-sucking flies represented by 1,347 species worldwide (Borkent and Dominiak 2020). Several spe- cies are of economic importa

ogonidae) are small, blood-sucking flies represented by 1,347 species worldwide (Borkent and Dominiak 2020). Several spe- cies are of economic importance due to their association with the transmission of pathogens to humans and livestock ( Meiswinkel et al. 2004) and biting nuisance (Linley and Davies 1971). Like other groups of insects, the species-level systematics of Culicoides is based primarily on descriptions of morphology which differ consistently between valid species. This primary approach to species delimitation can underestimate diversity when biolog - ically independent taxa cannot be adequately resolved by mor - phology alone ( Hebert et al. 2004 ). DNA barcode sequences of the mitochondrial cytochrome c oxidase I ( COI) 5 ʹ gene, used for generating comparative estimates of intraspecific and inter - specific genetic distances, can assist species-level resolution of animal taxa ( Hebert et al. 2004 ). DNA barcodes are increasingly reported as genetic evidence in support of species proposals in tax - onomic studies of Culicoides (Bellis et al. 2013, 2014). Conversely, Jou

asingly reported as genetic evidence in support of species proposals in tax - onomic studies of Culicoides (Bellis et al. 2013, 2014). Conversely, Journal of Medical Entomology, XX(XX), 2022, 1–11 https://doi.org/10.1093/jme/tjac142 Research Downloaded from https://academic.oup.com/jme/advance-article/doi/10.1093/jme/tjac142/6747045 by Chulalongkorn University user on 04 October 2022 2 Journal of Medical Entomology, 2022, Vol. XX, No. XX DNA barcoding can be used to flag potential presence of novel or cryptic diversity in geographically widespread taxa that are otherwise morphologically inseparable ( Gopurenko et al. 2015 ). DNA barcodes obtained from taxonomically identified specimens serve as standardized genetic references for species identification purposes. For example, voucher-associated DNA barcodes avail - able in public sequence repositories can be used in comparative genetic analyses to provide species identification of unknown specimens that lack diagnostic features due to life stage, gender, or sample degradation ( Gopurenko et al. 2015 ). DNA barcodes can also be used in

n specimens that lack diagnostic features due to life stage, gender, or sample degradation ( Gopurenko et al. 2015 ). DNA barcodes can also be used in diagnostic applications, including verifica - tion of identities of specimens from surveillance sampling ( Bellis et al. 2015 ) and facilitated identification of taxa by nonexpert entomologists (Harrup et al. 2015). A recent example of this utility was reported by Jomkumsing et al. (2021) who were able to iden - tify 90% of their specimens using DNA barcodes and which ulti - mately led to a new species description ( Pramual et al. 2021 ). The reliability of publicly available DNA barcodes can be problematic as few checks are in place to ensure sequence data contributed to repositories are accurate and have been associated with a correctly identified specimen. To partially manage this problem, publicly available DNA barcodes stored in the Barcode of Life Data systems (BOLD; http://boldsystems.org/ ; Ratnasingham and Hebert 2013 ) are passed through multiple checks, and information is available to indicate their level of compliance with

ems.org/ ; Ratnasingham and Hebert 2013 ) are passed through multiple checks, and information is available to indicate their level of compliance with BOLD quality criteria, in - cluding collection history details and critically if they are taxonom - ically identified/linked to vouchered specimens held at registered museums or similar agencies. The advent of nondestructive or partially destructive DNA ex - traction procedures ( Bellis et al. 2013 , Mathieu et al. 2020 ) goes some way to allowing retrospective alignment of DNA barcoding with morphological determinations, as researchers can lodge voucher specimens into collections for subsequent assessment of taxonomic identifications. Such assessments are important if DNA barcode libraries are to assist with the diagnostic identification of specimens or recognition of newly described taxa. Additionally, DNA barcode libraries inclusive of species sampled across their geographic ranges allow empirical genetic estimates of intraspecific diversity and inter- specific differences as a means to assess the accuracy of DNA bar - code identific

w empirical genetic estimates of intraspecific diversity and inter- specific differences as a means to assess the accuracy of DNA bar - code identifications (Bergsten et al. 2012). The application of DNA barcoding and other molecular techniques to taxonomic studies has assisted in the discovery of cryptic species which are morphologically inseparable but geneti - cally distinct (Bickford et al. 2007). The utility of DNA barcoding for flagging putative cryptic species diversity in taxonomic campaigns is well documented and has been applied for testing contentious spe - cies boundaries in Culicoides (e.g., Bellis et al. 2013, 2014). Using an integrative taxonomic approach, Gopurenko et al. (2015) proposed presence in Australasia of several potentially cryptic species within C. subg. Avaritia. These were delimited as genetically distinct con - specific groups, following analyses of independently inherited sequences from the DNA barcode region and a nuclear gene. Subtle morphological differences among conspecific groups were retrospec- tively identified in some instances as further suppo

ion and a nuclear gene. Subtle morphological differences among conspecific groups were retrospec- tively identified in some instances as further support of novel species diversity. DNA barcodes obtained from the type locality of the spe - cies can assist with delineating described species from putative novel taxa, particularly in cases where morphological analysis is not pos - sible, for example when the holotype specimen is missing, damaged or of the wrong sex. In these instances, retrospective morpholog - ical examinations of genetically different taxa that are supportive of proposed species delimitation can be used to assist with subsequent formal taxonomic descriptions. Gopurenko et al. (2015) determined that Culicoides species tend to exhibit <3% intraspecific variation (mitochondrial COI sequence p-distance). Conspecifics with higher levels of COI sequence vari - ation (>3%) were variably regarded as either putative cryptic spe - cies (if concordantly supported by other molecular or morphological evidence) or a single species with deep genetic differences, perhaps indicative of

cies (if concordantly supported by other molecular or morphological evidence) or a single species with deep genetic differences, perhaps indicative of the early stages of speciation. Given that intraspecific mtDNA genetic distances vary considerably among species (Fujita et al. 2012, Pilgrim et al. 2021, Shults et al. 2022), reliance on a single DNA barcode distance threshold as a criterion for species delimi - tation can lead to incorrect conclusions. Consequently, Gopurenko et al. (2015) recommended employing an integrative taxonomic approach to species delimitation (concordant data from several in - dependent genes and morphological analysis) guided by analysis of rapidly evolving DNA barcodes to initially flag presence of deep mtDNA divergence symptomatic of cryptic diversity in a described species. Flagging of potential cryptic species diversity in large DNA bar - code data sets can be accelerated using algorithms for delimitation of molecular operational taxonomic units directly from sample sequence data (Zhou et al. 2019 ). The Barcode Index Number (BIN) is one such system aut

of molecular operational taxonomic units directly from sample sequence data (Zhou et al. 2019 ). The Barcode Index Number (BIN) is one such system auto-implemented by BOLD (Ratnasingham and Hebert 2013), which provides an interim taxonomic framework of terminal genetic clusters of similar BOLD-compliant 5 ʹ COI DNA barcodes. BIN registration was initially designed to infer by association the taxo- nomic identities of DNA barcode records lacking a species-level iden- tification. More critically, DNA barcodes of identified species can be checked for taxonomic consistency with other BIN-associated DNA barcode records in BOLD providing a useful means for detection of species misidentifications, overlaps, and synonymies. Furthermore, registration of multiple BINs to a single species can signal the pres - ence of deep genetic variation among records, symptomatic of histor- ical population processes or overlooked species diversity. Despite the widespread adoption of DNA barcoding to assist in Culicoides taxonomy in many parts of the world, few such studies have been undertaken in Asia (Harr

widespread adoption of DNA barcoding to assist in Culicoides taxonomy in many parts of the world, few such studies have been undertaken in Asia (Harrup et al. 2015). Culicoides DNA barcodes or associated COI sequences have been sparsely reported from Japan ( Matsumoto et al. 2009 ), southern India ( Archana et al. 2015 , Harrup et al. 2016 ), southern China ( Liu et al. 2018 ), and Thailand ( Jomkumsing et al. 2021 ). Others have incorporated DNA barcode COI sequence data into systematic studies focused on a particular group of species present in Asia and surrounding re- gions (Bellis et al. 2014, Gopurenko et al. 2015, Mathieu et al. 2020). Independently reported DNA barcodes of some of the broadly dis - tributed species in Asia, collectively exhibit high genetic differences among sampled locations. This is generally evident in DNA barcode surveys which have sampled across biogeographic areas or included earlier reported sequence exemplars of species sampled elsewhere in Asia or adjoining regions ( Gopurenko et al. 2015 , Harrup et al. 2016). Not discounting possible taxonomic or D

plars of species sampled elsewhere in Asia or adjoining regions ( Gopurenko et al. 2015 , Harrup et al. 2016). Not discounting possible taxonomic or DNA barcode proc - essing errors, high levels of intraspecific DNA barcode diversity can undermine their diagnostic utility for providing taxonomically co - herent identifications of Culicoides from sparsely sampled areas, such as from within SE Asia. Sequence contributions of Cuilicoides species from across SE Asia, especially from type localities, will be invaluable and assist in efforts to understand the regional Culicoides diversity. The recent detection of African Horse Sickness in Thailand (King et al. 2020 ), a disease listed by the World Organisation for Animal Health (WOAH) and previously unknown from SE Asia, has prompted the need for information about the Culicodies spe- cies regarded as vectors of the pathogen causing the disease. There is consequently a need to enhance the existing capacity to identify Downloaded from https://academic.oup.com/jme/advance-article/doi/10.1093/jme/tjac142/6747045 by Chulalongkorn University use

ting capacity to identify Downloaded from https://academic.oup.com/jme/advance-article/doi/10.1093/jme/tjac142/6747045 by Chulalongkorn University user on 04 October 2022 3Journal of Medical Entomology, 2022, Vol. XX, No. XX specimens both within Thailand and in neighboring countries con - cerned about the spread of this disease. The Culicoides fauna of Thailand is relatively well studied with 100 species reported so far ( Thepparat et al. 2015 , Pramual et al. 2021), although DNA barcode sequences from specimens sampled from Thailand are currently only available for 18 of these species (Gopurenko et al. 2015 , Li et al. 2017 , Jomkumsing et al. 2021 ). This article reports progress in contributing to a COI DNA barcode library for Culicoides species in SE Asia by providing barcodes for 40 of the species present in Thailand. Materials and Methods Insects Culicoides specimens collected from 10 sites in three provinces of Thailand (Table 1) were preserved in >90% in ethanol and identified to species using the keys and descriptions of Wirth and Hubert (1989), Dyce et al. (2007) , and Bel

) were preserved in >90% in ethanol and identified to species using the keys and descriptions of Wirth and Hubert (1989), Dyce et al. (2007) , and Bellis (2020). A subset of 101 specimens that were representative of the collected species, were submitted for nondestructive DNA extraction ( Supp Methods S1 [online only ]). Extracted specimens were subsequently mounted onto glass slides following the methods of Bellis et al. (2013). Retrospective morpho- logical examinations of mounted specimens using afore-mentioned keys and descriptions and those of Yu et al. (2005) were conducted to confirm identifications. Vouchers of all specimens processed (listed in Supp Table S1 [online only]) are lodged in the National Arbovirus Monitoring Program Insect Collection (NAMPIC), Darwin, Australia and Ramkhamhaeng University, Bangkok, Thailand. DNA Barcoding COI Lysate from individual specimens was processed for DNA extraction and PCR amplification and sequence analysis of the mitochondrial 5ʹ COI DNA barcode region using methods adapted from Bellis et al. (2013) and reported in Supp Methods (online

sequence analysis of the mitochondrial 5ʹ COI DNA barcode region using methods adapted from Bellis et al. (2013) and reported in Supp Methods (online only). In summary, the DNA barcode region was targeted for PCR amplification using a variety of oligonucleotide primers ( Table 2). Primers BC1culicFm and JerR2m were paired in initial attempts to amplify 646-bp primer truncated DNA barcodes. Forward primer BC1culicFm was substituted by Tyr-Cul-Fm for improved amplification of full-length 5ʹ COI barcodes from some specimens. Similarly, the reverse primer JerR2m was used as a substitute for LEP-R1m. Partial-length DNA barcodes were targeted when full-length barcodes could not be ade- quately amplified from specimens. For this, BC1culicFm and reverse primer AMbc5r1m were paired to target a 329-bp PCR product that overlapped (89 bp) with a 406-bp PCR product amplified separately using forward primer AMbc3f1m paired with JerR2m. All primers include 5ʹ forward or reverse M13 sequence tails for simplified bi - directional Sanger sequencing at the Australian Genome Research Facility, Brisbane

nclude 5ʹ forward or reverse M13 sequence tails for simplified bi - directional Sanger sequencing at the Australian Genome Research Facility, Brisbane, Australia. Bidirectional sequence chromatograms were compiled, quality checked, and aligned as reported in Bellis et al. (2013). BioEdit ver. 7.0.9.0 (Hall 1999) was used to examine the align - ment of primer truncated DNA barcode sequences for symptoms of confounding pseudogenes, evidenced by the presence of se - quence alignment indels, frameshifts, and COI stop codons. DNA barcodes and associated specimen records including finalized identifications were publicly released as a public dataset at BOLD (DS-CULITHAI). Sequences were also submitted to the National Centre for Biotechnology Information (NCBI) and released with GenBank accession numbers (refer Supp Table S1 [online only ] for BOLD specimen records and associated GenBank accession numbers). uences and all pub - licly released DNA barcodes from BOLD followed the allocation of our sequences into the BOLD Barcode Index Number (BIN) system ( Ratnasingham and Hebert 2013 ). We also searched BOLD for other BINs associated with the species identified in our sample. This allowed us to compare our generated BINs to those reported earlier, as a means to summa - rize intraspecific genetic diversity, and to flag the presence of po - tentially cryptic diversity and/or misidentified species in earlier reported BINs. In total, 230 public DNA barcode sequences re - ported to BINs were obtained from BOLD, including a represen - tative subset of Thailand Culicoides sequences ( N = 60) reported by Jomkumsing et al. (2021) and Pramual et al. (2021 ;

BOLD, including a represen - tative subset of Thailand Culicoides sequences ( N = 60) reported by Jomkumsing et al. (2021) and Pramual et al. (2021 ; Supp Table S2 [online only ]). Pairwise genetic distances between all DNA barcodes were compared as an optimal neighbor-joining (NJ) tree, constructed using MEGA X ( Kumar et al. 2018 ). NJ cluster supports were estimated by bootstrap replication ( N = 10,000), and terminal clusters of taxa were compared to taxa clustered in BINs. No attempts were made to infer interspecific phylogenetic relationships from the NJ analyses. All genetic distance measures reported for summary comparisons, BINs and the NJ tree were estimated as the proportion of different nucleotides in pairwise comparisons of sequences, incorporating pairwise deletions of missing sites. Results We recovered DNA barcode sequences from 78 of the 101 Culicodes specimens representing 40 recognized species (Supp Table S1 [online only]). Of the 78 sequences, 58 exceeded 500 bp and were regarded as full-length sequences by BOLD. The remaining 20 sequences were regarded as partial

nly]). Of the 78 sequences, 58 exceeded 500 bp and were regarded as full-length sequences by BOLD. The remaining 20 sequences were regarded as partial sequences and although BOLD accepts sequences exceeding 300 bp, such sequences are excluded from formal BIN assignments. We found no evidence of pseudogenes among the DNA barcode sequences. DNA barcodes for 17 Culicoides species are pro- vided here for the first time (Tables 3 and 4). In total, 69 of the 78 generated sequences were deemed DNA barcode compliant by BOLD and registered into 39 public DNA barcode BINs, referable to 35 species ( Table 3). Twenty-three BINs were unique to Thailand representing 21 species ( Table 3). The re- maining 16 BINs were concordantly matched to records for 15 spe- cies sampled elsewhere in Australasia or Asia ( Table 3). Apart from BIN AAJ7131 that consisted of C. peregrinus sequences that differed by up to 3.19%, the maximum level of intra-BIN sequence difference did not exceed 3% in any of the remaining 38 BINs registered to our DNA barcodes. Nearest neighbor conspecific BIN distances varied widely,

difference did not exceed 3% in any of the remaining 38 BINs registered to our DNA barcodes. Nearest neighbor conspecific BIN distances varied widely, ranging from 1.63% for C. palpifer, to greater than 6% se - quence difference in four species (C. brevipalpis, C. circumbasalis, C. insignipennis, and C. jacobsoni). Species identities for 25 of the 35 nearest neighbor BINs remain unreported to the public. Ten unique but partial-length DNA barcode sequences (<410 bp) that were excluded from formal BIN assignment by BOLD due to their short lengths, were obtained from specimens that were mor - phologically identified to nine species (Table 4). Seven of these partial DNA barcode sequences were less than 1.68% different to sequences in BINs reported to species that matched with our morphological identifications (Table 4). Partial DNA barcodes we obtained from C. bubalus and C. kinari were novel species reports in BOLD and were unmatched to existing sequences in that repository. A partial DNA barcode sequence of a specimen identified as C. jacobsoni (ww20930) was unmatched in BOLD and minim

isting sequences in that repository. A partial DNA barcode sequence of a specimen identified as C. jacobsoni (ww20930) was unmatched in BOLD and minimally differed by 7.89% to C. jacobsoni sequences in BIN AAI9869 sampled from several Indo- West Pacific locations. A partial DNA barcode of a specimen of C. guttifer de Meijere was matched at >98.7% to DNA barcodes in BIN ACY3173 containing specimens of C. guttifer reported earlier from Thailand ( Jomkumsing et al. 2021 ) and Bangladesh. Collectively, these differed by 12.98% from BIN AAZ3810 comprising a single record of C. guttifer sampled from Malaysia. The partial DNA barcode of a specimen we identified as C. elbeli was unmatched to existing sequences at public repositories. An existing DNA barcode obtained from a Malaysian specimen of C. elbeli was placed into BIN ACT2936 along with our specimen (ww20947; Table 3 and Supp Fig. S1 [online only]) that we identified as C. menglaensis, using the taxonomic keys of Yu et al. (2005). Genetic distance relationships among DNA barcodes and BINs allocated to our sampled species and of previou

ing the taxonomic keys of Yu et al. (2005). Genetic distance relationships among DNA barcodes and BINs allocated to our sampled species and of previously released con - specific records are summarized in Table 5 and presented as an optimal NJ tree in Supp Fig. S1 (online only ). Specimen sequences allocated to BINs were supported as terminal groups of clustered DNA barcodes in the NJ tree. The BINs of 16 species exceeded 3% difference from conspecific records available in BOLD, five of which were intermediately distant (3–6% difference) and the remaining 11 species were more than 6% different (Table 5). A specimen of C. actoni we sampled from Thailand was assigned to BIN ADT6683 which is present in four other countries including the country of the type locality (India). This BIN differed by 3.77% from C. actoni BIN AAJ7360 reported previously from Thailand and seven other countries. One specimen we identified as C. huffi was assigned its own BIN (ACT5145) and was placed into a large clade with eight other publicly available BINs reported for this species, four of which were reported

s own BIN (ACT5145) and was placed into a large clade with eight other publicly available BINs reported for this species, four of which were reported previously from Thailand (Fig. 1A). The nearest BIN to our specimen was ADX2422 obtained from a spec - imen from China and which differed by >12.25% to the sequence of our specimen. Culicoides jacobsoni in Thailand is represented by two BINs, one of which (AAI9869) is present in eight other countries, and the other (ACP1813) is also present in Malaysia. Six other BINs have been assigned to DNA barocodes of this species from various locations in Australasia and all BINs differ by >5.3% from these two BINs present in Thailand (Gopurenko et al. 2015). Two of the three BINs assigned to C. palawanensis DNA barcodes are represented exclusively by specimens from Thailand. The minimum genetic distance between DNA barcodes of the three BINS’s exceeded 4%. Three specimens from Thailand identified as C. tamada were placed into two separate BINs which genetically differed by 15.79%. All three of these specimens were collected at the same location i

C. tamada were placed into two separate BINs which genetically differed by 15.79%. All three of these specimens were collected at the same location in Tambon Bang Sak. Four specimens from Thailand identified as C. palpifer were assigned into three BINs, two of which were previously reported from Timor Leste, while the other was novel (Fig. 1B). Two specimens from Thailand identified as C. parahumeralis were placed into 2 BINs, one of which, ACG0219, is reported in BOLD as a sequence from C. palpifer sampled from India ( Fig. 1B ). Similarly, DNA barcodes of specimens we identied as C. asiana , C. jacobsoni , C. hegneri, and C. actoni were allocated to individual BINs consisting of specimens reported to belong to two or more species (Table 3). Discussion This study provides a significant step in establishing a DNA bar - code library for all species of Culicoides present in Thailand. One of these species, C. cordiger, is here reported as a new species record for Thailand, bringing the total number of species reported for the country to 101. Subsequent efforts should thus aim at generating DNA barcodes for the species not yet represented on BOLD or similar libraries, and just as importantly, to increase specimen sampling effort needed to identify intraspecific genetic diversity across geographic domains (Bergsten et al. 2012). DNA barcodes for three species, C. circumbasalis , C. hegneri , and C. palpifer which were previously only available from specimens sourced outside of Thailand, are now available for specimens from Thailand. These DNA barcode sequences, however, are assigned to new BINs for these species, and are >6% different from other public sequences available for the taxa ( Table 3), suggesting either the existence of cryptic species or, as discussed below, issues with mis

m other public sequences available for the taxa ( Table 3), suggesting either the existence of cryptic species or, as discussed below, issues with misidentified specimens. NJ distance analysis supports this assertion as several of these sequence BINs do not group with BINs containing earlier reported sequences from conspecific specimens ( Supp Fig. 1 [online only]). Twenty-one of the species reported in the present study are represented by a single-specimen DNA barcode. Eleven of these DNA barcodes are assigned to BINs containing conspecific specimens; however, the remaining DNA barcodes are novel singleton se - quence records for 10 species. Species represented by a single bar - code have limited usefulness in assisting with DNA barcode based identifications for two reasons. To this end, we encourage workers lodging sequence data to use a nondestructive (e.g., Bellis et al. 2013) or partially destructive (e.g., Mathieu et al. 2020) nucleic acid extraction method allowing retro- spective examination of preserved specimens donated to a collection facility for curation. A further inconsistency raised in this study is the status of C. menglaensis and C. elbeli. The match between our specimen of C. menglaensis with data on BOLD for C. elbeli suggests some disparity between the morphological identification of the two specimens that produced these data. The sequence from the Malaysian specimen of C. elbeli on BOLD (specimen number BIOUG37346-B06) belongs to the same BIN as our specimen from Thailand bearing cibarial ar- mature so it can be assumed that the morphology of the Malaysian specimen is consistent with our specimen and also bears cibarial armature. This specimen was collected in Selangor, Malaysia, which is close to the type locality of C. elbeli so raises the possibility that this specimen is referable to C. elbeli sensu Wirth & Hubert (1959) rather than C. elbeli sensu Chu & Liu (1978) . An analysis of sequence data and morphological comparison of Asian specimens of C. brevitarsis sensu lato revealed that all records from Japan, including those of Matsumoto et al. As an example of this type of follow-through, several C. arakawae Arakawa accessions submitted to GenBank by Jomkumsing et al. (2021) were later assigned by Pramual et al. ubmitted to GenBank by Jomkumsing et al. (2021) were later assigned by Pramual et al. (2021) to their newly described species, C. mahasarakhamense Pramual et al. 2021 . Those accessions were retrospectively changed on GenBank by the original submitters, to reflect this updated taxonomy. Despite efforts by BOLD administration to set community standards for record submission and to provide compliance systems for spec - imen record/sequence checking, it is still subject to submitter errors which can confound sequence repository search engines. Furthermore, as BOLD actively mines COI records from GenBank for inclusion in its own database, inaccurate records obtained from GenBank are passed to BOLD without meeting the compli - ance standards required by BOLD (although users of GenBank and BOLD can flag putative incorrectly reported records with comments for investigative correction). We encourage researchers who have deposited sequence accessions to BOLD or GenBank to retrospectively update species names of their records if they are found to be doubtful, incorrect, placed under synonymy o

sions to BOLD or GenBank to retrospectively update species names of their records if they are found to be doubtful, incorrect, placed under synonymy or subject to other nomenclatural adjustments since their initial deposition. Similarly, we encourage researchers to contact sequence repository administrators, to report public accessions deemed to be in need of revision, as a means of bringing them to the attention of the original submitters. Furthermore, we recommend the use of the BOLD BIN system to researchers preparing DNA barcodes of vouchered species for public release. The BIN system can summarily alert record submitters to the presence of taxonomic incongruencies between their sequence records and publicly reported DNA barcodes. The BIN system also provides a useful means to flag genetically deep and potentially novel diversity in described species. Some of these, for example, C. brevipalpis , C huffi , C. palpifer , and C. jacobsoni , are already known to harbor cryptic species ( Bellis et al. 2015 , Gopurenko et al. 2015 , Li et al. 2017 ). Others, for example BIN ACL6375 containing C. hegneri, require confirmation of the iden - tification of all specimens currently placed into genetically dis - tant BINs. In species complexes, it is often difficult to determine which of the cryptic species is a valid representative of the desig - nated type specimen. This might be resolved if subtle morpholog - ical characters are present and can be confirmed by assessing the type specimens. However, type specimens are often inadequately preserved, damaged or even missing, exacerbating the problem. A possible solution is to investigate the genetic diversity of the species at the type locality. If the variation is limited, designation is warranted. The presence of more than one of the crypti

etic diversity of the species at the type locality. If the variation is limited, designation is warranted. The presence of more than one of the cryptic species at the locality will complicate matters and alternative justifications should be sought. Thailand contains the type localities of 3 spe - cies included in the present study viz C. gewertzi, C. huffi , and C. niphanae and one of these, C. huffi , was identified in this study as comprising several BINs that are symptomatic of deep intra - specific genetic diversity and or presence of cryptic species. There are, however, three BINs evident in this species in specimens from Thailand ( Fig. 1A), and we suggest an integrative taxonomic ap - proach to resolving the status of taxa in these BINs which should ideally involve morphological examination of the type specimen and sequence data of further material from the type locality of Bangkok. We recovered COI DNA barcode sequences from sixteen spe - cies that had not been previously reported in BOLD or GenBank. All these sequences also represented novel BINs or, in the case of C. kinari

teen spe - cies that had not been previously reported in BOLD or GenBank. All these sequences also represented novel BINs or, in the case of C. kinari and C. bubalus, where a BIN was not able to be assigned, formed a unique molecular taxonomic unit in a NJ distance tree (Supp Fig. 1 [online only]). Some of these, for example, C. brevipalpis and C. palpifer , are both individually suspected of being species complexes (Bellis et al. 2014, Gopurenko et al. 2015). Our specimens of these species from Thailand were placed within BINs identified by the two earlier studies, and so do not represent further novel genetic diversity. Most of the DNA barcodes generated from Thai specimens have been collected around livestock (e.g., Jomkumsing et al. 2021 ) and so represent the most common livestock pest species present in Thailand. As such, the burgeoning DNA barcode library is likely to be comprehensive enough to be of assistance to future studies of livestock pests in Thailand. Abstract Recent focus on Culicoides species diversity in Thailand was prompted by a need to identify vectors respon- sible for the transmission of African Horse Sickness in that country. To assist rapid genetic identification of spe- cies, we sampled mitochondrial cytochrome c oxidase subunit I (COI) DNA barcodes (N = 78) from 40 species of Culicoides biting midge from Thailand, including 17 species for which DNA barcodes were previously una- vailable. The DNA barcodes were assigned to 39 Barcode Identification Numbers (BINs) representing terminal genetic clusters at the Barcode of Life Data systems (BOLD). BINs assisted with comparisons to published conspecific DNA barcodes and allowed partial barcodes obtained from seven specimens to be associated with BINs by their similarity. Some taxonomic issues were revealed and attributed to the possible misiden

tained from seven specimens to be associated with BINs by their similarity. Some taxonomic issues were revealed and attributed to the possible misidentification of earlier reported specimens as well as a potential synonymy of C. elbeli Wirth & Hubert and C. menglaensis Chu & Liu. Comparison with published BINs also revealed genetic evidence of divergent population processes and or potentially cryptic species in 16 described taxa, flagged by their high levels of COI sequence differ - ence among conspecifics. We recommend the BOLD BIN system to researchers preparing DNA barcodes of vouchered species for public release. This will alert them to taxonomic incongruencies between their records and publicly released DNA barcodes, and also flag genetically deep and potentially novel diversity in described species. Keywords: biting midge, Barcode Index Number, cryptic species, vector Biting midges of the genus Culicoides Latreille (Diptera: Ceratopogonidae) are small, blood-sucking flies represented by 1,347 species worldwide (Borkent and Dominiak 2020). Several spe- cies are of economic impor

opogonidae) are small, blood-sucking flies represented by 1,347 species worldwide (Borkent and Dominiak 2020). Several spe- cies are of economic importance due to their association with the transmission of pathogens to humans and livestock (Meiswinkel et al. 2004) and biting nuisance (Linley and Davies 1971). Like other groups of insects, the species-level systematics of Culicoides is based primarily on descriptions of morphology which differ consistently between valid species. This primary approach to species delimitation can underestimate diversity when biolog- ically independent taxa cannot be adequately resolved by mor - phology alone (Hebert et al. 2004). DNA barcode sequences of the mitochondrial cytochrome c oxidase I (COI) 5ʹ gene, used for generating comparative estimates of intraspecific and inter - specific genetic distances, can assist species-level resolution of animal taxa (Hebert et al. 2004). DNA barcodes are increasingly reported as genetic evidence in support of species proposals in tax- onomic studies of Culicoides (Bellis et al. 2013, 2014). Conversely, Journal of

y reported as genetic evidence in support of species proposals in tax- onomic studies of Culicoides (Bellis et al. 2013, 2014). DNA barcodes obtained from taxonomically identified specimens serve as standardized genetic references for species identification purposes. For example, voucher-associated DNA barcodes avail- able in public sequence repositories can be used in comparative genetic analyses to provide species identification of unknown specimens that lack diagnostic features due to life stage, gender, or sample degradation (Gopurenko et al. 2015). DNA barcodes can also be use

unknown specimens that lack diagnostic features due to life stage, gender, or sample degradation (Gopurenko et al. 2015). DNA barcodes can also be used in diagnostic applications, including verifica- tion of identities of specimens from surveillance sampling (Bellis et al. 2015) and facilitated identification of taxa by nonexpert entomologists (Harrup et al. 2015). A recent example of this utility was reported by Jomkumsing et al. (2021) who were able to iden- tify 90% of their specimens using DNA barcodes and which ulti- mately led to a new species description (Pramual et al. 2021). The reliability of publicly available DNA barcodes can be problematic as few checks are in place to ensure sequence data contributed to repositories are accurate and have been associated with a correctly identified specimen. To partially manage this problem, publicly available DNA barcodes stored in the Barcode of Life Data systems (BOLD; http://boldsystems.org/; Ratnasingham and Hebert 2013) are passed through multiple checks, and information is available to indicate their level of compliance with BOLD

.org/; Ratnasingham and Hebert 2013) are passed through multiple checks, and information is available to indicate their level of compliance with BOLD quality criteria, in- cluding collection history details and critically if they are taxonom- ically identified/linked to vouchered specimens held at registered museums or similar agencies. The advent of nondestructive or partially destructive DNA ex- traction procedures (Bellis et al. 2013, Mathieu et al. 2020) goes some way to allowing retrospective alignment of DNA barcoding with morphological determinations, as researchers can lodge voucher specimens into collections for subsequent assessment of taxonomic identifications. Such assessments are important if DNA barcode libraries are to assist with the diagnostic identification of specimens or recognition of newly described taxa. Additionally, DNA barcode libraries inclusive of species sampled across their geographic ranges allow empirical genetic estimates of intraspecific diversity and inter- specific differences as a means to assess the accuracy of DNA bar - code identifications (Ber

genetic estimates of intraspecific diversity and inter- specific differences as a means to assess the accuracy of DNA bar - code identifications (Bergsten et al. 2012). The application of DNA barcoding and other molecular techniques to taxonomic studies has assisted in the discovery of cryptic species which are morphologically inseparable but geneti- cally distinct (Bickford et al. 2007). The utility of DNA barcoding for flagging putative cryptic species diversity in taxonomic campaigns is well documented and has been applied for testing contentious spe- cies boundaries in Culicoides (e.g., Bellis et al. 2013, 2014). Using an integrative taxonomic approach, Gopurenko et al. (2015) proposed presence in Australasia of several potentially cryptic species within C. subg. Avaritia. These were delimited as genetically distinct con- specific groups, following analyses of independently inherited sequences from the DNA barcode region and a nuclear gene. Subtle morphological differences among conspecific groups were retrospec- tively identified in some instances as further support of novel sp

ear gene. Subtle morphological differences among conspecific groups were retrospec- tively identified in some instances as further support of novel species diversity. DNA barcodes obtained from the type locality of the spe- cies can assist with delineating described species from putative novel taxa, particularly in cases where morphological analysis is not pos- sible, for example when the holotype specimen is missing, damaged or of the wrong sex. In these instances, retrospective morpholog- ical examinations of genetically different taxa that are supportive of proposed species delimitation can be used to assist with subsequent formal taxonomic descriptions. Gopurenko et al. (2015) determined that Culicoides species tend to exhibit <3% intraspecific variation (mitochondrial COI sequence p-distance). Conspecifics with higher levels of COI sequence vari- ation (>3%) were variably regarded as either putative cryptic spe- cies (if concordantly supported by other molecular or morphological evidence) or a single species with deep genetic differences, perhaps indicative of the early stages o

ly supported by other molecular or morphological evidence) or a single species with deep genetic differences, perhaps indicative of the early stages of speciation. Given that intraspecific mtDNA genetic distances vary considerably among species (Fujita et al. 2012, Pilgrim et al. 2021, Shults et al. 2022), reliance on a single DNA barcode distance threshold as a criterion for species delimi- tation can lead to incorrect conclusions. Consequently, Gopurenko et al. (2015) recommended employing an integrative taxonomic approach to species delimitation (concordant data from several in- dependent genes and morphological analysis) guided by analysis of rapidly evolving DNA barcodes to initially flag presence of deep mtDNA divergence symptomatic of cryptic diversity in a described species. Flagging of potential cryptic species diversity in large DNA bar - code data sets can be accelerated using algorithms for delimitation of molecular operational taxonomic units directly from sample sequence data (Zhou et al. 2019). The Barcode Index Number (BIN) is one such system auto-implemented by BOLD

nal taxonomic units directly from sample sequence data (Zhou et al. 2019). The Barcode Index Number (BIN) is one such system auto-implemented by BOLD (Ratnasingham and Hebert 2013), which provides an interim taxonomic framework of terminal genetic clusters of similar BOLD-compliant 5ʹ COI DNA barcodes. BIN registration was initially designed to infer by association the taxo- nomic identities of DNA barcode records lacking a species-level iden- tification. More critically, DNA barcodes of identified species can be checked for taxonomic consistency with other BIN-associated DNA barcode records in BOLD providing a useful means for detection of species misidentifications, overlaps, and synonymies. Furthermore, registration of multiple BINs to a single species can signal the pres- ence of deep genetic variation among records, symptomatic of histor- ical population processes or overlooked species diversity. Despite the widespread adoption of DNA barcoding to assist in Culicoides taxonomy in many parts of the world, few such studies have been undertaken in Asia (Harrup et al. 2015). Culicoi

DNA barcoding to assist in Culicoides taxonomy in many parts of the world, few such studies have been undertaken in Asia (Harrup et al. 2015). Culicoides DNA barcodes or associated COI sequences have been sparsely reported from Japan (Matsumoto et al. 2009), southern India (Archana et al. 2015, Harrup et al. 2016), southern China (Liu et al. 2018), and Thailand (Jomkumsing et al. 2021). Others have incorporated DNA barcode COI sequence data into systematic studies focused on a particular group of species present in Asia and surrounding re- gions (Bellis et al. 2014, Gopurenko et al. 2015, Mathieu et al. 2020). Independently reported DNA barcodes of some of the broadly dis- tributed species in Asia, collectively exhibit high genetic differences among sampled locations. This is generally evident in DNA barcode surveys which have sampled across biogeographic areas or included earlier reported sequence exemplars of species sampled elsewhere in Asia or adjoining regions (Gopurenko et al. 2015, Harrup et al. 2016). Not discounting possible taxonomic or DNA barcode proc- essing errors, high

in Asia or adjoining regions (Gopurenko et al. 2015, Harrup et al. 2016). Not discounting possible taxonomic or DNA barcode proc- essing errors, high levels of intraspecific DNA barcode diversity can undermine their diagnostic utility for providing taxonomically co- herent identifications of Culicoides from sparsely sampled areas, such as from within SE Asia. Sequence contributions of Cuilicoides species from across SE Asia, especially from type localities, will be invaluable and assist in efforts to understand the regional Culicoides diversity. The recent detection of African Horse Sickness in Thailand (King et al. 2020), a disease listed by the World Organisation for Animal Health (WOAH) and previously unknown from SE Asia, has prompted the need for information about the Culicodies spe- cies regarded as vectors of the pathogen causing the disease. The Culicoides fauna of Thailand is relatively well studied with 100 species reported so far (Thepparat et al. 2015, Pramual et al. 2021), although DNA barcode sequences from specimens sampled from Thailand are currently only available for 18 of these species (Gopurenko et al. 2015, Li et al. 2017, Jomkumsing et al. 2021). This article reports progress in contributing to a COI DNA barcode library for Culicoides species in SE Asia by providing barcodes for 40 of the species present in Thailand. Materials and Methods Insects Culicoides specimens collected from 10 sites in three provinces of Thailand (Table 1) were preserved in >90% in ethanol and identified to species using the keys and descriptions of Wirth and Hubert (1989), Dyce et al. (2007), and Bellis (2020). A subset of 101 specimens that were representative of the collected s

descriptions of Wirth and Hubert (1989), Dyce et al. (2007), and Bellis (2020). A subset of 101 specimens that were representative of the collected species, were submitted for nondestructive DNA extraction (Supp Methods S1 [online only]). Extracted specimens were subsequently mounted onto glass slides following the methods of Bellis et al. (2013). Retrospective morpho- logical examinations of mounted specimens using afore-mentioned keys and descriptions and those of Yu et al. (2005) were conducted to confirm identifications. Vouchers of all specimens processed (listed in Supp Table S1 [online only]) are lodged in the National Arbovirus Monitoring Program Insect Collection (NAMPIC), Darwin, Australia and Ramkhamhaeng University, Bangkok, Thailand. DNA Barcoding COI Lysate from individual specimens was processed for DNA extraction and PCR amplification and sequence analysis of the mitochondrial 5ʹ COI DNA barcode region using methods adapted from Bellis et al. (2013) and reported in Supp Methods (online only). In summary, the DNA barcode region was targeted for PCR amplification using

pted from Bellis et al. (2013) and reported in Supp Methods (online only). In summary, the DNA barcode region was targeted for PCR amplification using a variety of oligonucleotide primers (Table 2). Primers BC1culicFm and JerR2m were paired in initial attempts to amplify 646-bp primer truncated DNA barcodes. Forward primer BC1culicFm was substituted by Tyr-Cul-Fm for improved amplification of full-length 5ʹ COI barcodes from some specimens. Similarly, the reverse primer JerR2m was used as a substitute for LEP-R1m. Partial-length DNA barcodes were targeted when full-length barcodes could not be ade- quately amplified from specimens. For this, BC1culicFm and reverse primer AMbc5r1m were paired to target a 329-bp PCR product that overlapped (89 bp) with a 406-bp PCR product amplified separately using forward primer AMbc3f1m paired with JerR2m. All primers include 5ʹ forward or reverse M13 sequence tails for simplified bi- directional Sanger sequencing at the Australian Genome Research Facility, Brisbane, Australia. Bidirectional sequence chromatograms were compiled, quality checked, and

r sequencing at the Australian Genome Research Facility, Brisbane, Australia. Bidirectional sequence chromatograms were compiled, quality checked, and aligned as reported in Bellis et al. (2013). BioEdit ver. 7.0.9.0 (Hall 1999) was used to examine the align- ment of primer truncated DNA barcode sequences for symptoms of confounding pseudogenes, evidenced by the presence of se- quence alignment indels, frameshifts, and COI stop codons. DNA barcodes and associated specimen records including finalized identifications were publicly released as a public dataset at BOLD (DS-CULITHAI). Sequences were also submitted to the National Centre for Biotechnology Information (NCBI) and released with GenBank accession numbers (refer Supp Table S1 [online only] for BOLD specimen records and associated GenBank accession numbers). Comparisons among our DNA barcode sequences and all pub- licly released DNA barcodes from BOLD followed the allocation of our sequences into the BOLD Barcode Index Number (BIN) system (Ratnasingham and Hebert 2013). We also searched BOLD for other BINs associated with the species identified in our sample. et al. (2021; Supp Table S2 [online only]). Pairwise genetic distances between all DNA barcodes were compared as an optimal neighbor-joining (NJ) tree, constructed using MEGA X (Kumar et al. 2018). NJ cluster supports were estimated by bootstrap replication (N = 10,000), and terminal clusters of taxa were compared to taxa clustered in BINs. No attempts were made to infer interspecific phylogenetic relationships from the NJ analyses. All genetic distance measures reported for summary comparisons, BINs and the NJ tree were estimated as the proportion of different nucleotides in pairwise comparisons of sequences, incorporating pairwise deletions of missing sites. Results We recovered DNA barcode sequences from 78 of the 101 Culicodes specimens representing 40 recognized species (Supp Table S1 [online only]). Of the 78 sequences, 58 exceeded 500 bp and were regarded as full-length sequences by BOLD. The remaining 20 sequences were regarded as partial sequences and although BOLD accepts sequences exceeding 300 bp, such sequences are excluded from formal BIN assignments. We found no evide

as partial sequences and although BOLD accepts sequences exceeding 300 bp, such sequences are excluded from formal BIN assignments. We found no evidence of pseudogenes among the DNA barcode sequences. DNA barcodes for 17 Culicoides species are pro- vided here for the first time (Tables 3 and 4). In total, 69 of the 78 generated sequences were deemed DNA barcode compliant by BOLD and registered into 39 public DNA barcode BINs, referable to 35 species (Table 3). Twenty-three BINs were unique to Thailand representing 21 species (Table 3). The re- maining 16 BINs were concordantly matched to records for 15 spe- cies sampled elsewhere in Australasia or Asia (Table 3). Apart from BIN AAJ7131 that consisted of C. peregrinus sequences that differed by up to 3.19%, the maximum level of intra-BIN sequence difference did not exceed 3% in any of the remaining 38 BINs registered to our DNA barcodes. Nearest neighbor conspecific BIN distances varied widely, ranging from 1.63% for C. palpifer, to greater than 6% se- quence difference in four species (C. brevipalpis, C. circumbasalis, C. insignipen

widely, ranging from 1.63% for C. palpifer, to greater than 6% se- quence difference in four species (C. brevipalpis, C. circumbasalis, C. insignipennis, and C. jacobsoni). Species identities for 25 of the 35 nearest neighbor BINs remain unreported to the public. Ten unique but partial-length DNA barcode sequences (<410 bp) that were excluded from formal BIN assignment by BOLD due to their short lengths, were obtained from specimens that were mor - phologically identified to nine species (Table 4). Seven of these partial DNA barcode sequences were less than 1.68% different to sequences in BINs reported to species that matched with our morphological identifications (Table 4). Partial DNA barcodes we obtained from C. bubalus and C. kinari were novel species reports in BOLD and were unmatched to existing sequences in that repository. A partial DNA barcode sequence of a specimen identified as C. jacobsoni (ww20930) was unmatched in BOLD and minimally differed by 7.89% to C. jacobsoni sequences in BIN AAI9869 sampled from several Indo- West Pacific locations. A partial DNA barcode of a s

d minimally differed by 7.89% to C. jacobsoni sequences in BIN AAI9869 sampled from several Indo- West Pacific locations. A partial DNA barcode of a specimen of C. guttifer de Meijere was matched at >98.7% to DNA barcodes in BIN ACY3173 containing specimens of C. guttifer reported earlier from Thailand (Jomkumsing et al. 2021) and Bangladesh. Collectively, these differed by 12.98% from BIN AAZ3810 comprising a single record of C. guttifer sampled from Malaysia. The partial DNA barcode of a specimen we identified as C. elbeli was unmatched to existing sequences at public repositories. An existing DNA barcode obtained from a Malaysian specimen of C. elbeli was placed into BIN ACT2936 along with our specimen (ww20947; Table 3 and Supp Fig. S1 [online only]) that we identified as C. menglaensis, using the taxonomic keys of Yu et al. (2005). Genetic distance relationships among DNA barcodes and BINs allocated to our sampled species and of previously released con- specific records are summarized in Table 5 and presented as an optimal NJ tree in Supp Fig. S1 (online only). Specimen sequence

eviously released con- specific records are summarized in Table 5 and presented as an optimal NJ tree in Supp Fig. S1 (online only). Specimen sequences allocated to BINs were supported as terminal groups of clustered DNA barcodes in the NJ tree. The BINs of 16 species exceeded 3% difference from conspecific records available in BOLD, five of which were intermediately distant (3–6% difference) and the remaining 11 species were more than 6% different (Table 5). A specimen of C. actoni we sampled from Thailand was assigned to BIN ADT6683 which is present in four other countries including the country of the type locality (India). This BIN differed by 3.77% from C. actoni BIN AAJ7360 reported previously from Thailand and seven other countries. One specimen we identified as C. huffi was assigned its own BIN (ACT5145) and was placed into a large clade with eight other publicly available BINs reported for this species, four of which were reported previously from Thailand (Fig. 1A). The nearest BIN to our specimen was ADX2422 obtained from a spec- imen from China and which differed by >12.25%

ed previously from Thailand (Fig. 1A). The nearest BIN to our specimen was ADX2422 obtained from a spec- imen from China and which differed by >12.25% to the sequence of our specimen. Culicoides jacobsoni in Thailand is represented by two BINs, one of which (AAI9869) is present in eight other countries, and the other (ACP1813) is also present in Malaysia. Six other BINs have been assigned to DNA barocodes of this species from various locations in Australasia and all BINs differ by >5.3% from these two BINs present in Thailand (Gopurenko et al. 2015). Two of the three BINs assigned to C. palawanensis DNA barcodes are represented exclusively by specimens from Thailand. The minimum genetic distance between DNA barcodes of the three BINS’s exceeded 4%. Three specimens from Thailand identified as C. tamada were placed into two separate BINs which genetically differed by 15.79%. All three of these specimens were collected at the same location in Tambon Bang Sak. Four specimens from Thailand identified as C. palpifer were assigned into three BINs, two of which were previously reported from

in Tambon Bang Sak. Four specimens from Thailand identified as C. palpifer were assigned into three BINs, two of which were previously reported from Timor Leste, while the other was novel (Fig. 1B). Two specimens from Thailand identified as C. parahumeralis were placed into 2 BINs, one of which, ACG0219, is reported in BOLD as a sequence from C. palpifer sampled from India (Fig. 1B). Similarly, DNA barcodes of specimens we identied as C. asiana, C. jacobsoni, C. hegneri, and C. actoni were allocated to individual BINs consisting of specimens reported to belong to two or more species (Table 3). Discussion This study provides a significant step in establishing a DNA bar - code library for all species of Culicoides present in Thailand. One of these species, C. cordiger, is here reported as a new species record for Thailand, bringing the total number of species reported for the country to 101. Including this contribution, 59 of the 101 species recorded from Thailand are now represented by one or more DNA barcodes. However, only 47 of these 59 DNA barcoded species were Downloaded from ht

Subsequent efforts should thus aim at generating DNA barcodes for the species not yet represented on BOLD or similar libraries, and just as importantly, to increase specimen sampling effort needed to identify intraspecific genetic diversity across geographic domains (Bergsten et al. 2012). DNA barcodes for three species, C. circumbasalis, C. hegneri, and C. palpifer which were previously only available from specimens sourced outside of Thailand, are now available for specimens from Thailand. These DNA barcode sequences, however, are assigned to new BINs for these species, and are >6% different from other public sequences available for the taxa (Table 3), suggesting either the existence of cryptic species or, as discussed below, issues with misidentified specimens. NJ distance analysis supports this assertion as several of these sequence BINs do not group with BINs containing earlier reported sequences from conspecific specimens (Supp Fig. 1 [online only]). Twenty-o

l of these sequence BINs do not group with BINs containing earlier reported sequences from conspecific specimens (Supp Fig. 1 [online only]). Twenty-one of the species reported in the present study are represented by a single-specimen DNA barcode. Eleven of these DNA barcodes are assigned to BINs containing conspecific specimens; however, the remaining DNA barcodes are novel singleton se- quence records for 10 species. Species represented by a single bar - code have limited usefulness in assisting with DNA barcode based identifications for two reasons. To this end, we encourage workers lodging sequence data to use a nondestructive (e.g., Bellis et al. 2013) or partially destructive (e.g., Mathieu et al. 2020) nucleic acid extraction method allowing retro- spective examination of preserved specimens donated to a collection facility for curation. Where retrospective examination is not pos- sible, the addition of more sequence data from reliably identified specimens should help to clarify the actual species associated with these BINs. A further inconsistency raised in this study is the status of C. menglaensis and C. elbeli. The match between our specimen of C. menglaensis with data on BOLD for C. elbeli suggests some disparity between the morphological identification of the two specimens that produced these data. Culicoides menglaensis is morphologically similar to C. elbeli differing, according to Chu and Liu (1978) and Yu et al. (2005), mainly in the presence of cibarial armature in C. menglaensis. Wirth and Hubert (1959, 1989), however, do not refer to this character in their descriptions of C. elbeli and Chu and Liu (1978) did not state whether or not they examined any specimens of C. elbeli sensu Wirth and Hubert (1959) to confirm the absence of cibarial armature. r not they examined any specimens of C. elbeli sensu Wirth and Hubert (1959) to confirm the absence of cibarial armature. The sequence from the Malaysian specimen of C. elbeli on BOLD (specimen number BIOUG37346-B06) belongs to the same BIN as our specimen from Thailand bearing cibarial ar- mature so it can be assumed that the morphology of the Malaysian specimen is consistent with our specimen and also bears cibarial armature. This specimen was collected in Selangor, Malaysia, which is close to the type locality of C. elbeli so raises the possibility that this specimen is referable to C. elbeli sensu Wirth & Hubert (1959) rather than C. elbeli sensu Chu & Liu (1978). The status of C. menglaensis is consequently in doubt until the holotype specimen of C. elbeli can be examined for the presence of cibarial armature. The two public records of C. brevitarsis reported by Matsumoto et al. (2009) have been placed by BOLD into BIN AAI9876 containing 52 sequences from C. asiana This species was con- fused with C. brevitarsis until well after Matsumoto et al. (2009) published their sequence d

aining 52 sequences from C. asiana This species was con- fused with C. brevitarsis until well after Matsumoto et al. (2009) published their sequence data (Bellis et al. 2014). An analysis of sequence data and morphological comparison of Asian specimens of C. brevitarsis sensu lato revealed that all records from Japan, including those of Matsumoto et al. (2009), are referable to C. asiana ( Bellis et al. 2014). These two sequences should ideally be renamed C. asiana to reflect the true identity of these specimens. As an example of this type of follow-through, several C. arakawae Arakawa accessions submitted to GenBank by Jomkumsing et al. (2021) were later assigned by Pramual et al. (2021) to their newly described species, C. mahasarakhamense Pramual et al. 2021. Those accessions were retrospectively changed on GenBank by the original submitters, to reflect this updated taxonomy. Despite efforts by BOLD administration to set community standards for record submission and to provide compliance systems for spec- imen record/sequence checking, it is still subject to submitter errors which

andards for record submission and to provide compliance systems for spec- imen record/sequence checking, it is still subject to submitter errors which can confound sequence repository search engines. Furthermore, as BOLD actively mines COI records from GenBank for inclusion in its own database, inaccurate records obtained from GenBank are passed to BOLD without meeting the compli- ance standards required by BOLD (although users of GenBank and BOLD can flag putative incorrectly reported records with comments for investigative correction). We encourage researchers who have deposited sequence accessions to BOLD or GenBank to retrospectively update species names of their records if they are found to be doubtful, incorrect, placed under synonymy or subject to other nomenclatural adjustments since their initial deposition. Similarly, we encourage researchers to contact sequence repository administrators, to report public accessions deemed to be in need of revision, as a means of bringing them to the attention of the original submitters. Furthermore, we recommend the use of the BOLD BIN sys

be in need of revision, as a means of bringing them to the attention of the original submitters. Furthermore, we recommend the use of the BOLD BIN system to researchers preparing DNA barcodes of vouchered species for public release. The BIN system can summarily alert record submitters to the presence of taxonomic incongruencies between their sequence records and publicly reported DNA barcodes. The BIN system also provides a useful means to flag genetically deep and potentially novel diversity in described species. The application of molecular tools to the taxonomy of Culicoides continues to assist with the detection of cryptic species with a further 11 species identified herein which comprise multiple BINs which differ by more than 6% (Table 5). Some of these, for example, C. brevipalpis, C huffi, C. palpifer, and C. jacobsoni, are already known to harbor cryptic species (Bellis et al. 2015, Gopurenko et al. 2015, Li et al. 2017). Others, for example BIN ACL6375 containing C. hegneri, require confirmation of the iden- tification of all specimens currently placed into genetically dis-

s, for example BIN ACL6375 containing C. hegneri, require confirmation of the iden- tification of all specimens currently placed into genetically dis- tant BINs. In species complexes, it is often difficult to determine which of the cryptic species is a valid representative of the desig- nated type specimen. This might be resolved if subtle morpholog- ical characters are present and can be confirmed by assessing the type specimens. However, type specimens are often inadequately preserved, damaged or even missing, exacerbating the problem. A possible solution is to investigate the genetic diversity of the species at the type locality. If the variation is limited, designation is warranted. The presence of more than one of the cryptic species at the locality will complicate matters and alternative justifications should be sought. Thailand contains the type localities of 3 spe- cies included in the present study viz C. gewertzi, C. huffi, and C. niphanae and one of these, C. huffi, was identified in this study as comprising several BINs that are symptomatic of deep intra- specific genetic

d C. niphanae and one of these, C. huffi, was identified in this study as comprising several BINs that are symptomatic of deep intra- specific genetic diversity and or presence of cryptic species. There are, however, three BINs evident in this species in specimens from Thailand (Fig. 1A), and we suggest an integrative taxonomic ap- proach to resolving the status of taxa in these BINs which should ideally involve morphological examination of the type specimen and sequence data of further material from the type locality of Bangkok. We recovered COI DNA barcode sequences from sixteen spe- cies that had not been previously reported in BOLD or GenBank. All these sequences also represented novel BINs or, in the case of C. kinari and C. bubalus, where a BIN was not able to be assigned, formed a unique molecular taxonomic unit in a NJ distance tree (Supp Fig. 1 [online only]). Some of these, for example, C. brevipalpis and C. palpifer, are both individually suspected of being species complexes (Bellis et al. 2014, Gopurenko et al. 2015). Our specimens of these species from Thailand were plac

individually suspected of being species complexes (Bellis et al. 2014, Gopurenko et al. 2015). Our specimens of these species from Thailand were placed within BINs identified by the two earlier studies, and so do not represent further novel genetic diversity. Most of the DNA barcodes generated from Thai specimens have been collected around livestock (e.g., Jomkumsing et al. 2021) and so represent the most common livestock pest species present in Thailand. As such, the burgeoning DNA barcode library is likely to be comprehensive enough to be of assistance to future studies of livestock pests in Thailand. Further contributions to this library are needed to improve both its robustness and comprehensiveness and this will likely require collecting specimens from other habitats, for example, the coastal habitats sampled during this study but Downloaded from https://academic.oup.com/jme/article/59/6/1960/6747045 by Preeya porn user on 30 June 2025 1969 Journal of Medical Entomology, 2022, Vol. 59, No. 6 also native forests, swamps, etc., and from other hosts to produce specimens of unsampl

content: la.ac.th Simple Summary: Biting midges, which feed on a variety of hosts, transmit a wide range of harmful human and animal viruses and parasites. According to recent studies in Thailand, biting midges may act as potential vectors for leishmaniasis and trypanosomiasis. The purpose of this study is to look for Leishmania and Trypanosoma DNA in biting midges obtained in the field in a leishmaniasis- endemic area in Phatthalung province, Southern Thailand. In addition, we analysed engorged midges for host blood DNA and screened the collected midges for avian haemosporidian parasites. According to our findings, biting midges have a diversified feeding habit and may be able to transmit various pathogens, including trypanosomatid and avian haemosporidian parasites. Abstract: Five hundred and fifty-nine female biting midges were collected , and seventeen species in six subgenera ( Avaritia, Haemophoructus, Hoffmania, Meijerehelea, Remmia , and Trithecoides) and two groups ( Clavipalpis and Shortti) were identified. The dominant Culicoides species was C. peregrinus (30.94%), followed by C. s
source: 2022-Molecular Identification Host Blood Meals Parasites Insects

content: Trithecoides) and two groups ( Clavipalpis and Shortti) were identified. The dominant Culicoides species was C. peregrinus (30.94%), followed by C. subgenus Trithecoides. From blood meal analysis of engorged biting midges , they were found to feed on cows, dogs, pigs, and avians. The majority of blood preferences of biting midges (68%; 49/72 ) displayed a mixed pattern of host blood DNA (cow and avian). The overall non-engorged biting midge field infectivity rate was 1.44% (7/487). We detected Leucocytozoon sp. in three Culicoides specimens, one from each species: C. fulvus, C. oxystoma, and C. subgenus Trithecoides. Crithidia sp. was found in two C. peregrinus specimens, and Trypanosoma sp. and P. juxtanucleare were separately found in two C. guttifer. More consideration should be paid to the capacity of biting midges to transmit pathogens su ch as avian haemosporidian and trypanosomatid parasites. To demonstrate that these biting midges are natural vectors of trypanosomatid parasites, additional research must be conducted with a greater number of biting midges in other endemic reg

Keywords: biting midges; blood meal; Haemosporidians; Trypanosomatids; Thailand 1. Introduction The rising prevalence of vector -borne parasitic infections has raised global health awareness. Some zoonotic infections can be transmitted to humans by blood -sucking vectors that routinely feed on other animals. Insects 2022, 13, 912 2 of 16 vector-borne parasitic diseases that their potential vectors, Culicoides biting midges, also feed on humans [1]. Leishmaniasis is an emerging infectious disease in Thailand, caused by the Leishmania parasite, an obligate intracellular protozoon. The main cl inical presentations of leishmaniasis can be divided into three forms: visceral leishmaniasis (VL), mucocutaneous leishmaniasis (MCL), and cutaneous leishmaniasis (CL). All cases of leishmaniasis found in Thailand between 1960 and 1999 were brought in by i mmigrants or returning visitors from endemic areas. The first case of autochthonous leishmaniasis was reported in 1999 [2]. Since then, the reports of autochthonous leish
source: 2022-Molecular Identification Host Blood Meals Parasites Insects

content: ng visitors from endemic areas. The first case of autochthonous leishmaniasis was reported in 1999 [2]. Since then, the reports of autochthonous leishmaniasis cases in humans have been increasing. To date, more than 20 cases of autochth onous leishmaniasis have been confirmed in Thailand. The three major species causing indigenous human leishmaniasis in Thailand include Leishmania martiniquensis , L. orientalis (previously reported “L. siamensis”), and L. infantum. Among these three specie s, the most frequently identified species is L. martiniquensis belonging to the subgenus Mundinia [3]. The majority of Leishmania parasite transmission occurred through the bite of infected female phlebotomine sand flies (Diptera: Psychodidae), except for Mundinia. Natural vectors of the recently discovered Leishmania subgenus remain inconclusive. Recent studies have uncovered significant evidence that the Culicoides biting midge (Diptera: Ceratopogonidae) could act as a vector of leishmaniasis caused by Mundinia species consisting of L. martiniquensis, L. orientalis, and L. sp. from Ghana [4]. Hae
source: 2022-Molecular Identification Host Blood Meals Parasites Insects

content: idae) could act as a vector of leishmaniasis caused by Mundinia species consisting of L. martiniquensis, L. orientalis, and L. sp. from Ghana [4]. Haemosporidian parasites, order Haemosporidia, infect a wide range of vertebrate species all over the world. These intracellular blood parasites are divided into three genera: Leucocytozoon, Plasmodium, and Haemoproteus. Insects primarily serve as their vectors, in which mosquitoes (Culicidae), black flies (Simuliidae), and biting midges (Ceratopogonidae) are vectors of Plasmodium, Leucocytozoon, and Haemoproteus, respectively [5]. There are roughly 1,400 Culicoides biting midge species that have been reported throughout the world [6]. Only a few species, however, act as disease vectors in both animals and humans. The biting midges, genus Culicoides, not only play a vital role in the transmission of avian Haemosporidia parasites, which can cause lethal avian malaria in non-adapted birds and domestic chickens but also of avian Trypanosoma [7]. Culicoides blood meal analyses help researchers understand more about the behaviour of these insec
source: 2022-Molecular Identification Host Blood Meals Parasites Insects

content: omestic chickens but also of avian Trypanosoma [7]. Culicoides blood meal analyses help researchers understand more about the behaviour of these insects and the possibility of zoonotic disease transmission from animals to human beings. Earlier studies found that the Culicoides biting midges feed on a variety of hosts, including mammals and birds [8]. Due to the favourable climate for both sand fly and biting midge growth and development, the southern and northern parts of Thailand are now regarded as key endemic locations of autochthonous leishmaniasis [9]. Despite ongoing reports of leishmaniasis cases caused by Mundinia species in Thailand, the established vectors remain unknown [10]. Other studies from Thailand suggest that sand flies and biting midges are both potential vectors for autochthonous leishmaniasis and trypanosomiasis [9,11,12]. The study, conducted in Lamphun province, Northern Thailand, near the residence of a leishmaniasis patient, found L. martiniquensis and avian Trypanosoma DNA in collected biting midges, highlighting the role of Culicoides biting midges as poten
source: 2022-Molecular Identification Host Blood Meals Parasites Insects

content: asis patient, found L. martiniquensis and avian Trypanosoma DNA in collected biting midges, highlighting the role of Culicoides biting midges as potential vectors of both autochthonous leishmaniasis and trypanosomiasis [9]. The aim of the study is to investigate the Leishmania and Trypanosoma parasites in Culicoides biting midges collected from the affected area of autochthonous leishmaniasis in Phatthalung province, southern Thailand. Other pathogens that biting midges may act as possible vectors for, such as haemosporidians, were also tested. Additionally, in order to comprehend the interactions between vectors and hosts, the Culicoides host-feeding preferences were also investigated. Insects 2022, 13, 912 3 of 16 2. Materials and Methods 2.1. Ethics Statement The investigation protocol on animals was approved by the animal research ethics committee of Chulalongkorn University Animal Care and Use Protocol (CU -ACUP), Faculty of Medicine, Chulalongkorn University, Bangkok, Thailand (COA No. 019/2563). 2.2. Biting Midge Collection Biting midges were trapped in the surroundings of the autochthonous leishmaniasis patient’s home . This study was conducted in Tamot District, Phatthalung Province of Southern Thailand (7° 19′51.34″N 100° 6′4.3″E) in October 2020. These flies were collected by using Center for Disease Control and Prevention miniature light traps with ultraviolet light (CDC-UV light). Four traps were placed 1.5 m above the ground around the patient’s home (Figure 1) and were continuously set up from 6.00 pm to 6.00 am for two nights. The collected insects in the bags were anesthetized by cooling on ice for 30 min. Subsequently, the female biting midges were morphologically separated from other insects under a stereomicroscope (Olympus, Tokyo, Japan). Figure 1. Engorged biting midges were used for the host blood meal identification, while non - engorged biting midges were screened for parasitic infections. 2.3. DNA Isolation Total DNA was isolated from the thorax and abdomen of individual biting midges. The flies were lysed in 100 µL of lysis buffer supplemented with 10 µL of proteinase K and grounded with a sterilized plastic pestle. DNA isolation was performed using an Invisorb® Spin Tissue mini kit (STRATEC Molecular GmbH, Berlin, Germany) according to the manufacturer’s instructions. The DNA was eluted with 40 µL of elution buffer and then stored at −20 ° C until use. 2.5. Sequence Analysis and Phylogenetic Tree Construction All positive amplified products were ligated into the pGEM -T® Easy Vector (Promega, Mandison, WI, USA) acco rding to the manufacturer’s instructions. Plasmid DNA containing the suspected insert gene was extracted using the GeneAll ® ExprepTM Plasmid purification kit (GeneAll Biotechnology, co., ltd, Seoul, Korea) following the manufacturer’s instructions. content: logy, co., ltd, Seoul, Korea) following the manufacturer’s instructions. The purified plasmid DNA was submitted to the Macrogen Inc. (Seoul, South Korea) for sequencing service. The obtained nucleotide sequences were manually edited to achieve the consensus sequences before alignment using ClustalW multiple alignments in BioEdit Sequence Alignment Editor Version 7.2.5 [19]. The consensus nucleotide sequences were then compared to the sequences available in the GenBank database using the nucleotide BLAST tool (https://blast.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/Blast.cgi, accessed on 23 June 2022). The phylogenetic tree was generated by MEGAX software [20] based on the maximum likelihood method with 1000 bootstrap replicates. The generated SSU rRNA sequences were submitted to the GenBank database under the accession numbers OP217134 for Trypanosoma sp. and OP217135 and OP217136 for Crithidia species. The haemosporidian cytb sequences were deposited in GenBank (accession no. OP227012 for P. juxtanucleare; OP227013-OP227015 for Leucocytozoon sp.). 3. Results 3.1. Species Composition of Biting Midges A total of 559 female Culicoides biting midges were collected, belonging to 17 species from 6 subgenera, consisting of Avaritia, Haemophoructus, Hoffmania, Meijerehelea, Remmia, Trithecoides, and two groups of Shortti and Clavipalpis (Table 2). Of these, 72 (12.88%) were blood-engorged. Based on morphological identification, C. peregrinus (30.94%) was the most abundant Culicoides species found in the study area, followed by C. subgenus Trithecoides (24.87%). In addition, for the subgenus Trithecoides, the specimens could not be identified at the species level and, therefore, were identified by their morphologic characteristics, possessing a yellow scutum and three spermathecae.
