ing primers S. Sunantaraporn et al. Current Research in Parasitology & Vector-Borne Diseases 9 (2026) 100387 2 C1-J-1718 and C1-N-2191 ( Dallas et al., 2003 ), and the PCR amplifi - cation protocol was performed as described by Mathieu et al. (2020) . 2.4. PCR identification of Wolbachia and Cardinium in field-caught Culicoides spp. The presence of Wolbachia and Cardinium endosymbionts was assessed through PCR amplification of specific gene fragments. For Wolbachia , a fragment of the wsp ( Wolbachia surface protein) gene was amplified using the forward primer wsp81F (5 ′ -TGG TCC AAT AAG TGA TGA AGA AAC-3 ′ ) and the reverse primer wsp691R (5 ′ -AAA AAT TAA ACG CTA CTC CA-3 ′ ) ( Zhou et al., 1998 ). Detection of Cardinium was performed by amplifying a highly conserved region of the 16S rRNA gene with primers CAR-SP-F (5 ′ -CGG CTT ATT AAG TCA GTT GTG AAA TCC TAG-3 ′ ) and CAR-SP-R (5 ′ -TCC TTC CTC CCG CTT ACA CG-3 ′ ) ( Nakamura et al., 2009 ). Each PCR reaction master mix was performed in a total volume of 25 μ l containing 6 μ l of genomic DNA (approxi - mately 5 – 10 ng), 12.5

l., 2009 ). Each PCR reaction master mix was performed in a total volume of 25 μ l containing 6 μ l of genomic DNA (approxi - mately 5 – 10 ng), 12.5 μ l of Green Hot Start PCR Master Mix Direct-Load 2 × (Biotechrabbit, Berlin, Germany), 1 μ l of each forward and reverse primer (10 μ M), and 4.5 μ l of sterile deionized water. The thermal cycling conditions included an initial denaturation at 95 ◦ C for 3 min, followed by 35 cycles of denaturation at 95 ◦ C for 1 min, annealing at 55 ◦ C for wsp and 57 ◦ C for 16S rRNA for 1 min, and extension at 72 ◦ C for 1 min, with a final extension step at 72 ◦ C for 5 min. Plasmid DNA containing the specific sizes of the wsp and 16S rRNA genes served as positive controls, while genomic DNA from Culicoides specimens confirmed to be free of Wolbachia and Cardinium , along with sterile deionized water, were included as negative controls. The expected size of the wsp PCR product is approximately 593 – 644 bp, depending on the Wolbachia strain, while that of the Cardinium PCR product is approxi - mately 595 bp. 2.5. Detection of Leishmania DNA in Cu

644 bp, depending on the Wolbachia strain, while that of the Cardinium PCR product is approxi - mately 595 bp. 2.5. Detection of Leishmania DNA in Culicoides spp. The presence of Leishmania DNA was detected using a conventional PCR assay targeting the internal transcribed spacer 1 (ITS1) region of the ribosomal DNA cluster. Amplification was performed with the forward primer LeF (5 ′ -TCC GCC CGA AAG TTC ACC GAT A-3 ′ ) and the reverse primer LeR (5 ′ -CCA AGT CAT CCA TCG CGA CAC G-3 ′ ) ( Spanakos et al., 2008 ). PCR reactions were carried out using the Green Hot Start PCR Master Mix Direct-Load 2 × (Biotechrabbit, Berlin, Germany) according to the manufacturer ’ s instructions. The PCR cycling conditions followed the protocols previously described by Srisuton et al. (2019) and Sunantaraporn et al. (2021) . DNA extracted from Leishmania martini - quensis culture (MHOM/TH/2012/CULE1) served as the positive con - trol, while male Culicoides and sterilized deionized water were used as the negative control. 2.6. DNA cloning and sequencing The PCR products for Leishmania spp., Wolbachia

ides and sterilized deionized water were used as the negative control. 2.6. DNA cloning and sequencing The PCR products for Leishmania spp., Wolbachia , and Cardinium were inserted into the pGEM-T Easy Vector (Promega, Madison, WI, USA) using a rapid DNA ligation kit (Promega, Madison, WI, USA) following the manufacturer ’ s instructions. The plasmid DNA was transformed into Escherichia coli (DH5 α ) competent cells, and the transformants were screened by blue-white colony selection using a colony PCR assay. Three to five positive clones, presumed to contain the desired genes, were cultured in Luria-Bertani (LB) broth, with the Fig. 1. The chimeric DNA was extracted using the GeneAll ® Exprep ™ plasmid purification kit (GeneAll, Seoul, Korea) following the manufacturer ’ s instructions. The extracted DNA was then sent to a commercial service at Macrogen Inc., South Korea, for Sanger DNA sequencing. 2.7. Phylogenetic tree construction and haplotype network All nucleotide sequences were subjected to manual editing and trimming prior to alignment using the Clustal W multiple alignment function in BioEdit sequence alignment editor v.7.2.5 ( Hall, 1999 ). The consensus nucleotide sequences were compared to previously published sequences in the GenBank database using the Basic Local Alignment Search Tool (BLAST) online in the NCBI database ( https://blast.ncbi.nl m.nih.gov/Blast.cgi ). The phylogenetic relationships were recon - structed using the maximum likelihood (ML) method, based on the model with the lowest Bayesian Information Criteri

ogenetic relationships were recon - structed using the maximum likelihood (ML) method, based on the model with the lowest Bayesian Information Criterion (BIC) scores, as implemented in MEGA 11 ( Tamura et al., 2021 ). The reliability of the phylogenetic tree was estimated based on 1000 bootstrap pseudor - eplicates. The Wolbachia putative strain prediction was evaluated based on the analysis of the wsp sequences using the DnaSP software version 6 ( Rozas et al., 2017 ). A Templeton-Crandall-Sing (TCS) network was constructed using PopART v.1.7 ( Leigh and Bryant, 2015 ). 2.8. Statistical analysis Species richness (the number of species in the study areas) and species relative abundance (the number of samples per species/total number of samples × 100) for each site were calculated. Prevalence was calculated as the proportion of PCR-positive samples relative to the total number of individuals examined for each Culicoides species, study site, and infection status (single infection and co-infection). Prevalences are presented as percentages with corresponding 95% confidence intervals (95

te, and infection status (single infection and co-infection). Prevalences are presented as percentages with corresponding 95% confidence intervals (95% CI). The associations between the detection of Leishmania DNA and locations, Culicoides species, and endosymbiont presence were assessed using binary logistic regression. A P -value of < 0.05 was defined as statistically significant. All statistical analyses were performed using SPSS v.29 (IBM Corp., Armonk, NY, USA). 3. Results 3.1. Species composition of Culicoides biting midges A total of 593 specimens of Culicoides , representing 21 species of Culicoides and one species of Culicoides ( Trithecoides ), were collected from five collection sites across Songkhla (SK1, SK2, and SK3) in the southern region and Chiang Rai and Lampang in the northern region of Thailand. Species richness of Culicoides spp. demonstrated the lowest richness observed in SK1 (5 species) and the highest in Lampang (16 species). Overall species richness across all sampling locations was 21 species. The relative abundance of Culicoides spp. differed across sam -

(16 species). Overall species richness across all sampling locations was 21 species. The relative abundance of Culicoides spp. differed across sam - pling locations. Lampang contributed the high relative abundance of Culicoides samples (36.76%; 218/593), followed by Chiang Rai (26.98%; 160/593). In southern Thailand, SK2 showed the highest relative abundance (17.87%; 106/593), followed by SK3 (13.49%; 80/593) and SK1 (4.89%; 29/593). Culicoides peregrinus was the most frequent species ( n = 127), accounting for 21.4% of all collected samples, followed by C. guttifer ( n = 78), C. orientalis ( n = 59), C. oxystoma ( n = 48), and C. shortti ( n = 48). The Culicoides species composition varied among sampling sites, with distinct dominant species observed at each location. In SK1, C. oxystoma was the most abundant species ( n = 11; 37.9%). For SK2, C. peregrinus predominated ( n = 85; 80.2%), whereas SK3 was dominated by C. guttifer ( n = 49; 61.3%). Culicoides tainanus ( n = 35; 21.9%) and C. orientalis ( n = 34; 21.3%) were mainly found in Chiang Rai. In addition, C. shortti ( n = 42;

49; 61.3%). Culicoides tainanus ( n = 35; 21.9%) and C. orientalis ( n = 34; 21.3%) were mainly found in Chiang Rai. In addition, C. shortti ( n = 42; 19.3%) was the most frequently collected species in Lampang ( Table 1 and Fig. 1 ). 3.2. Detection of Wolbachia and Cardinium endosymbionts in Culicoides spp. Conventional PCR was used to amplify the Wolbachia surface protein ( wsp ) gene in several Culicoides spp. Overall, a single Wolbachia infection was detected in 18 Culicoides species, with a prevalence of 29.01% (95% CI: 25.49 – 32.79%). In Songkhla, the prevalence exhibited variation among the collection sites, with 34.48% (95% CI: 19.85 – 52.74%), 21.70% (95% CI: 14.86 – 30.52%), and 12.50% (95% CI: 6.74 – 21.69%) recorded at SK1, SK2, and SK3, respectively. In northern Thailand, the prevalence of Wolbachia infection was 31.88% (95% CI: 25.14 – 39.46%) in Chiang Rai and 35.78% (95% CI: 29.71 – 42.34%) in Lampang. The presence of Cardinium infection was detected through the amplification of the 16S rRNA gene. Among the 593 specimens exam - ined, 86 (14.50%, 95% CI: 11.89 – 17.57

ardinium infection was detected through the amplification of the 16S rRNA gene. Among the 593 specimens exam - ined, 86 (14.50%, 95% CI: 11.89 – 17.57%) were found to be positive for Cardinium . In Songkhla, the prevalences were 3.45% (95% CI: 0 – 18.63%), 10.38% (95% CI: 5.74 – 17.79%), and 48.75% (95% CI: 38.11 – 59.51%) at sampling locations SK1, SK2, and SK3, respectively. In northern Thailand, the prevalence of Cardinium infection was 1.25% (95% CI: 0 – 4.73%) in Chiang Rai and 15.14% (95% CI: 10.95 – 20.53%) in Lampang. Cardinium infection was most frequently detected in C. guttifer and C. mahasarakhamense . Furthermore, co-infection with Wolbachia and Cardinium endosym - bionts was observed in several Culicoides species, with an overall prev - alence of 11.47% (95% CI: 9.14 – 14.30%). Co-infections were identified at three collection sites: SK3 in Songkhla (13.75%, 95% CI: 7.68 – 23.14%), Chiang Rai (7.50%, 95% CI: 4.23 – 12.77%), and Lamp - ang (20.64%, 95% CI: 15.78 – 26.52%) ( Table 2 ). 3.3. BLAST analysis revealed that all wsp sequences were identical to those of Wolbachia bacterial endosymbionts, with sequence similarities ranging from 91.43% to 100% ( Supplementary Table S1 ). Phylogenetic analysis of these Wolbachia -positive sequences revealed their allocation to three major Wolbachia supergroups: Supergroup A ( n = 10), Supergroup B ( n = 36), and Supergroup F ( n = 6). In one sample from C. guttifer , a co- infection was observed with Wolbachia Supergroup B (isolate MGSS28- 26) and Supergroup F (isolate MGSS28-28). According to phylogenetic analysis, the majority of wsp sequences were assigned to Wolbachia Su - pergroup B and clustered into six putative strains: Wol-b1, Wol-b2, Wol- b3, Wol-b4, Wol-b5, and Wol-b6. Two putative strains, Wol-a1 and Wol- a2, belonged to Wolbachia Supergroup A, and one putative strain, Wol- f1, was classified within Supergroup F ( Fig. 2 ). Haplotype network analysis of the newly characterised Wolbachia strains and 35 strains previously reported by Zhou et al. (1998) demonstrated that the wsp sequences clustered into three Wolbachia putative strains, which is consistent with previous classifications. The first cluster corresponded to the w Kerlac strain ( Wolbachia of Kerria lacca ) from Wol-b4 and comprised endosymbionts from 10 Culicoides samples: C. peregrinus ( n = 3), C. guttifer ( n = 3), C. mahasarakhamense ( n = 2), C. oxystoma ( n = 1), and C. huffi ( n = 1) collected from Songkhla, and one each, C. shortti (LP54) and C. mahasarakhamense (LP82), from Lampang. These species included C. malayae (CRP29) and C. orientalis (CRP64) from Chiang Rai, and C. mahasarakhamense (MGSS26) and C. peregrinus (T1SS49) from Songkhla. The second cluster, correspond - ing to the w Beva_B strain ( Wolbachia of Bicyclus evadne ) from Wol-b3, was identified in C. orientalis ( n = 2) and C. tainanus ( n = 2) from Chiang Rai, and in six Culicoides spp. including C. oxystoma , C. guttifer , C. huffi , C. actoni , C. peregrinus , and C. insignipennis collected from Lampang. The third cluster, w CauA strain ( Wolbachia of Cadra cautella ) from Wol-a2, was identified in C. guttifer from Songkhla. tic relationships of Cardinium endosymbionts A total of 52 Cardinium -positive samples from 16 Culicoides spp. were randomly selected for 16S rRNA sequencing. BLAST analysis of these sequences against the GenBank database revealed 97.98 – 99.83% simi - larity with “ Candidatus Cardinium hertigii ” (GenBank: KR026922 ) pre - viously reported in Culicoides williwilli . Additionally, eleven 16S rRNA sequences from multiple Culicoides species showed 98.65 – 98.99% sim - ilarity with the Cardinium endosymbiont of Microzetorchestes emeryi (GenBank: MG889459) ( Supplementary Table S2 ). The phylogenetic analysis classified the Cardinium sequences into two major groups, designated as Group A and Group C. The majority of sequences ( n = 41) clustered within Cardinium Group C, which was further divided into two subgroups ( Fig. 4 ). Subgroup C1 comprised 22 Cardinium sequences detected in 9 Culicoides spp., including reference sequences from Cardinium previously reported in various Culicoides species. inium previously reported in various Culicoides species. Subgroup C2 contained 19 sequences identified in six Culicoides spp. from the present study ( Fig. 4 ). Discussion The present study investigates the prevalence of two bacterial en - dosymbionts, Wolbachia and Cardinium , in Culicoides biting midges collected from affected areas of autocht

gates the prevalence of two bacterial en - dosymbionts, Wolbachia and Cardinium , in Culicoides biting midges collected from affected areas of autochthonous leishmaniasis in north - ern and southern Thailand. The overall Wolbachia infection rate was 29.0%, indicating a moderately high prevalence in natural populations. This finding contrasts with previous reports showing lower Wolbachia prevalence rates in Culicoides species collected in Spain (6.2%) ( Pag `es et al., 2017 ) and Australia (6.4%) ( Mee et al., 2015 ). The prevalences of Wolbachia detected in this study are closely aligned with those previ - ously observed in the USA (22.0%) ( Covey et al., 2020 ). Wolbachia infection was detected in multiple Culicoides species, including C. mahasarakamense , C. peregrinus , C. oxystoma , C. guttifer , C. jacosoni , and C. orientalis . These species have also been previously identified as Leishmania -positive in areas with a documented history of leishmaniasis, as reported by Sunantaraporn et al. (2021) , Songumpai et al. (2022) , Ampol et al. (2024) , and Promrangsee et al. (2024) . A

story of leishmaniasis, as reported by Sunantaraporn et al. (2021) , Songumpai et al. (2022) , Ampol et al. (2024) , and Promrangsee et al. (2024) . As demonstrated in a previous report by Pag `es et al. (2017) , Wolbachia has been identified in C. imicola , C. obsoletus ( s.l .), and C. pulicaris ( s.l .), which are considered Palaearctic vectors of bluetongue and Schmallenberg diseases. Low-level Wolbachia infection was identified in 10 Culicoides species from Australia ( Mee et al., 2015 ). The Wolbachia infection was identified in C. crepuscularis , C. debilipalpis , C. edeni , C. haematopotus , C. insignis , C . venustus , and C. sonorensis from the USA ( Covey et al., 2020 ). The 16S rRNA, ftsZ , and wsp genes have been frequently employed as genetic markers for the classification of the eight supergroups (A to H) of Wolbachia ( Augustinos et al., 2011 ; Wang et al., 2014 ; Glowska et al., 2015 ). The most common Wolbachia supergroups in arthropods are A and B ( Breeuwer et al., 1992 ; Werren et al., 1995 ). Phylogenetic analysis revealed that the Wolbachia isolates detected in

oups in arthropods are A and B ( Breeuwer et al., 1992 ; Werren et al., 1995 ). Phylogenetic analysis revealed that the Wolbachia isolates detected in Culicoides spp. in the present study were genetically clustered into supergroups A, B, and F. The majority of our samples were phylogenetically separated into multiple clades within Supergroup B. Wolbachia sequences previously detected in Australian and Spanish Culicoides spp. were segregated into two distinct clades within Supergroup B ( Mee et al., 2015 ; Pag `es et al., 2017 ). The Wolbachia Supergroup F has been identified in filarial nematodes, cat fleas, termites, and bed bugs ( Driscoll et al., 2020 ; Zimmermann et al., 2021 ; Chebbah et al., 2023 ; Sinha et al., 2023 ). It was a notable finding of this study that some Wolbachia isolates from Culicoides spp. were detected in the Supergroup F. It is noteworthy that co-infection with supergroups B and F was observed in C. guttifer in Songkhla. The overall infection rate of Cardinium was 14.5%, detected across multiple Culicoides species, indicating a moderate prevalence in this st

Songkhla. The overall infection rate of Cardinium was 14.5%, detected across multiple Culicoides species, indicating a moderate prevalence in this study. Previous investigations have reported varying prevalences of Cardinium infection in different regions, including Australia (25.5%), Japan (16.0%), and Spain (1.1%) ( Nakamura et al., 2009 ; Mee et al., 2015 ; Pag `es et al., 2017 ). Previously, several reports have identified the Cardinium endosymbiont in relevant Palaearctic Culicoides species: C. imicola , C. pulicaris , and C. punctatus . A study by Pag `es et al. (2017) has demonstrated the presence of Cardinium infection in a wide range of C. obsoletus ( s.l .), C. festivipennis , C. flavipulicaris , C. haranti , C. maritimus , C. minutissimus , C . newsteadi , C. punctatus , and C. sahariensis . Cardinium infections were observed in several vector species, including C. imicola and the Pulicaris species complex ( C. pulicaris , C. bysta , C. newsteadi , and C. punctatus ) ( Pilgrim et al., 2021 ). The most prevalent Culicoides species infected with Cardinium was C. guttifer (9

. bysta , C. newsteadi , and C. punctatus ) ( Pilgrim et al., 2021 ). The most prevalent Culicoides species infected with Cardinium was C. guttifer (94.87%; 74/78), followed by C. mahasarakhamense (78.57%; 33/42). High Cardinium prevalence in Culicoides species is likely influenced by several factors. First, species-specific variation plays an important role, as previous studies have shown that certain species, such as C . imicola and members of the C. pulicaris complex, often exhibit moderate to high infection rates, whereas others, including the C . obsoletus species group, may display low or undetectable levels of infection ( Pilgrim et al., 2021 ). Three Culicoides species ( C. arakawae , C. ohmorii , and C. peregrinus ) exhibited a 100% Cardinium infection rate, as all examined individuals tested pos - itive ( Nakamura et al., 2009 ). Secondly, environmental factors have been implicated, with evidence suggesting that conditions such as land surface temperature are strongly associated with infection prevalence. In particular, higher temperatures during early developmental stages

h as land surface temperature are strongly associated with infection prevalence. In particular, higher temperatures during early developmental stages may promote increased infection frequencies in adult midges ( Morag et al., 2012 , 2013 ). Thirdly, habitat-related differences may also contribute to variation in prevalence. For instance, Culicoides pop - ulations collected near livestock premises have been reported to exhibit significantly higher Cardinium infection rates compared to those from more natural habitats, indicating that host-associated ecological factors may influence the distribution and maintenance of this endosymbiont ( Pag `es et al., 2017 ). S. Sunantaraporn et al. Current Research in Parasitology & Vector-Borne Diseases 9 (2026) 100387 10 may be shaped by a combination of habitat-related and species-specific factors. Notably, C. guttifer and C. mahasarakhamense were both collected from livestock-associated environments, specifically chicken coops ( Jomkumsing et al., 2021 ; Sunantaraporn et al., 2022 ; Tepboon - rueng et al., 2026 ), which may promote higher infection frequencies due to shared ecological conditions and increased opportunities for symbi - ont persistence and transmission. Accordingly, these species represent the most suitable candidates for examining the influence of Cardinium on vectorial capacity ( Pilgrim et al., 2021 ). The present study demonstrates the occ

st suitable candidates for examining the influence of Cardinium on vectorial capacity ( Pilgrim et al., 2021 ). The present study demonstrates the occurrence of co-infections with Wolbachia and Cardinium in several Culicoides species, with a moderate prevalence of 11.47%. Previous studies have reported substantially higher co-infection rates in other insect groups, such as planthoppers, where a prevalence of 32.7% has been observed ( Nakamura et al., 2009 ). However, to date, co-infection of these endosymbionts has not been reported in Culicoides biting midges. A phylogenetic analysis of Cardinium based on 16S rRNA se - quences revealed the existence of seven distinct groups: A, B, C, D, E, F, and G ( Tarlachkov et al., 2023 ). In the present study, the majority of Cardinium -positive samples were grouped in Group C, which contained Cardinium endosymbionts detected in diverse Culicoides species from regions worldwide. Furthermore, the 16S rRNA sequences of the Car - dinium strains were genetically grouped into two subgroups within Group C (C1 and C2). Furthermore, this is the first r

6S rRNA sequences of the Car - dinium strains were genetically grouped into two subgroups within Group C (C1 and C2). Furthermore, this is the first report of Cardinium being classified into Group A in Culicoides spp. A previous report has indicated that Cardinium Group A has been identified in several insect groups, including hemipterans, hymenopterans, opilionids, mites, and spiders ( Noel and Atibalentja, 2006 ). This study identified Wolbachia putative strains using a TCS haplo - type network, which illustrates the genealogical relationships among strains detected in host populations. Genetic variation (mutations) in Wolbachia surface protein ( wsp ) gene was analyzed to determine the relatedness among strains, infer ancestral lineages, and assess their distribution across different host species. The Wolbachia endosymbiont networks in Culicoides biting midges were grouped according to the Wolbachia putative strains previously described by Zhou et al. (1998) based on wsp sequences. In this study, three Wolbachia putative strains were identified as w Kerlac ( Wolbachia of Kerria la

bed by Zhou et al. (1998) based on wsp sequences. In this study, three Wolbachia putative strains were identified as w Kerlac ( Wolbachia of Kerria lacca ), w Beva_B ( Wol - bachia of Bicyclus evadne ), and w CauA ( Wolbachia of Cadra cautella ). The effects of Wolbachia infection are diverse and include inhibiting arbo - virus, malaria, and filarial nematode development, as well as inducing various reproductive abnormalities ( Stouthamer et al., 1999 ). The re - sults of this study suggest that the putative Wolbachia strains identified may play a role in determining sexual characteristics and the sex ratio of insect populations ( Sasaki et al., 2005 ; Vashishtha et al., 2011 ; Duplouy and Brattstr ¨om, 2018 ). In the present study, Wolbachia infection was classified solely based on the wsp gene. The separation of the clades into distinct Wolbachia Supergroup B sequences indicated a high degree of divergence, consistent with the expectation that the strain would be novel. To accurately characterize the novel Wolbachia strain, a multi-locus sequence typing (MLST) analysis should be co

tion that the strain would be novel. To accurately characterize the novel Wolbachia strain, a multi-locus sequence typing (MLST) analysis should be conducted using the gatB , coxA , hcpA , fbpA , and ftsZ genes ( Baldo et al., 2006 ). This approach will facilitate a more precise delineation of the Wolbachia strains. Detection of Leishmania parasites was carried out using an ITS1-PCR assay, revealing an overall prevalence of 8.94%. Although Leishmania DNA was identified using this molecular approach, these results do not confirm vector competence. A key limitation of PCR-based detection is its inability to distinguish between viable parasites and residual DNA originating from recent blood meals. Therefore, further studies, such as parasite isolation, cultivation, and experimental infection assays involving Leishmania and their suspected vectors, are necessary to evaluate their potential for transmission. This study demonstrated the co-occurrence of Leishmania parasites and endosymbionts, exhibiting distinct patterns of Wolbachia and Car - dinium infection. Additionally, it revealed th

currence of Leishmania parasites and endosymbionts, exhibiting distinct patterns of Wolbachia and Car - dinium infection. Additionally, it revealed the presence of double in - fections with Wolbachia and Cardinium . A research publication by Zhao et al. (2013) suggested that multiple infections of Cardinium and Wol - bachia affect host age through a combination of CI shared in the spider mite ( Tetranychus phaselus ). Nevertheless, the function of Cardinium infection in arthropod fitness remains uncertain. In Culicoides biting midges, Cardinium infection in C. imicola did not affect the survival rate under laboratory conditions ( Morag et al., 2013 ). The presence of Wolbachia supergroups A and B in sympatric infections was identified in Leishmania -positive samples. However, Wolbachia Supergroup F and co-infection with any strains were not identified in Leishmania -positive samples. Azpurua et al. (2010) demonstrated the occurrence of Fig. 5. Current Research in Parasitology & Vector-Borne Diseases 9 (2026) 100387 11 Leishmania and Wolbachia in Lutzomyia trapidoi. In contrast, previous reports of Wolbachia-positive sand flies and Leishmania DNA have demonstrated the absence of co-infection between the two organisms in sand flies ( Lozano-Sardaneta et al., 2022 ). Although the impact of Wolbachia on pathogen development, including viruses, filarial nema - todes, and malaria, has been examined in various insect species, there is currently no evidence of Wolbachia infection or a study of host-symbiont-pathogen interactions, particularly in Leishmania para - sites and their bacteria in biting midges. The present investigation demonstrates that endosymbiotic bacteria, including single infections with Wolbachia and Cardinium, as well as co- infection with both, are significantly associated with Leishmania DNA detection. We hypothesized that the presence of endosymbionts would

well as co- infection with both, are significantly associated with Leishmania DNA detection. We hypothesized that the presence of endosymbionts would be associated with negative detection of Leishmania DNA. However, both single and co-infections with Wolbachia and Cardinium were detected in samples that were either positive or negative for Leishmania DNA. These findings indicate no clear association between endosymbiont presence and Leishmania DNA detection in Culicoides spp. These findings highlight the complexity of tripartite associations among hosts, symbionts, and pathogens ( Schinkel et al., 2024 ). Further experimental and longitudinal studies are required to clarify the mechanical basis of these relationships and to determine whether endosymbionts directly affect Leishmania development or transmission. 5. Conclusions To the best of our knowledge, this is the first report of Wolbachia and Cardinium endosymbionts in field-collected Culicoides spp. from Leish - mania-affected areas in Thailand. In this study, Wolbachia belonging to supergroups A, B, and F were identified, and p

ed Culicoides spp. from Leish - mania-affected areas in Thailand. In this study, Wolbachia belonging to supergroups A, B, and F were identified, and putative strains including wKerlac, wBeva_B, and wCauA were characterized. Additionally, Cardi - nium of groups A and C was detected. Co-occurrence of these bacterial endosymbionts with Leishmania parasites was also demonstrated. These findings provide further evidence for the tripartite interactions among hosts, symbionts, and pathogens, with potential implications for vector control strategies. However, the functional roles and properties of Wolbachia and Cardinium in influencing vector fitness and pathogen transmission require further investigation. Research paper Trypanosomatid detection in Culicoides biting midges (Diptera: Ceratopogonidae) from leishmaniasis-endemic Songkhla Province, Southern Thailand: Microscopy and nanopore metabarcoding reveal parasite diversity Piyapat Tepboonrueng a , Paul Gabriel Lerona b , Chatchapon Srichareonsuk c , Chulaluk Promrangsee d , Rungfar Boonserm c , e , Rinnara Ampol c , e , Padet Siriyasatien c , e , Kanok Preativatanyou c , e , * a Medical Science Program, Faculty of Medicine, Chulalongkorn University, Bangkok 10330, Thailand b International Program of Biomedical Sciences and Biotechnology, Faculty of Medicine, Chulalongkorn University, Bangkok 10330, Thailand c Center of Excellence in Vector Biology and Vector-Borne Disease, Chulalongkorn University, Bangkok 10330, Thailand d Interdisciplinary Program of Biomedical Sciences, Graduate School, Chulalongkorn University, Bangkok 10330, Thailand e Department of Parasitology, Faculty of Medicine, Chulalongkorn University, Bangkok 10330, Thailand ARTICLE INFO Keywords: Leishmania Trypanosomatids Culicoides Avian trypanosome Microscopy Nanopo

ne, Chulalongkorn University, Bangkok 10330, Thailand ARTICLE INFO Keywords: Leishmania Trypanosomatids Culicoides Avian trypanosome Microscopy Nanopore metabarcoding ABSTRACT Leishmaniasis caused by Leishmania ( Mundinia ) spp. has recently emerged in Southeast Asia, including Thailand. Although molecular studies have detected Leishmania DNA in Culicoides midges, complete parasite development, which proves vector competence, remains undemonstrated in field specimens. We collected 215 parous/gravid ( n = 195) and blood-engorged ( n = 20) female Culicoides representing at least 10 species from two leishmaniasis foci in Songkhla Province, Southern Thailand. Specimens underwent microscopic dissection, culturing in Schneider's medium, ITS1/SSU rRNA-PCR for Leishmania /trypanosomatids, cox 1-PCR for blood meals, and MinION ® nanopore metabarcoding. Microscopy revealed live metacyclic trypomastigotes in one C. guttifer , which was confirmed as avian Trypanosoma bennetti -related by both culture and phylogenetics. No Leishmania promastigotes were observed, yet L. martiniquensis and L. orien

s avian Trypanosoma bennetti -related by both culture and phylogenetics. No Leishmania promastigotes were observed, yet L. martiniquensis and L. orientalis were molecularly detected in C. guttifer , C. huffi , C. ( Trithecoides ) spp., and C. orientalis with an overall prevalence of 16.3%. Trypanosoma bennetti -related sequences and monoxenous trypanosomatids ( Herpetomonas and Critidia ) were also identified (7.0% combined). Chickens were primary hosts for C. guttifer , C. huffi , and C. mahasarakhamense , with opportunistic human feeding in C. guttifer . These findings reveal that nanopore metabarcoding enables the detection of low-abundance, multi- parasite communities, the resolution of haplotype diversity, and the identification of blood meal sources. However, microscopy remains essential for differentiating viable trypanosomatid infections from non-viable, residual DNA that persists across gonotrophic cycles. While Leishmania DNA detection suggests that the midges feed on infected hosts, the absence of microscopically observable promastigotes prevents confirmation of vector com

detection suggests that the midges feed on infected hosts, the absence of microscopically observable promastigotes prevents confirmation of vector competence. Conversely, natural T. bennetti -related trypomastigotes confirm C. guttifer as a competent vector for avian trypanosomes. These findings establish an integrated microscopy-metabarcoding approach for trypanosomatid surveillance in Thai Culicoides , providing baseline data on parasite detection and host feeding patterns in emerging leishmaniasis foci. 1. Introduction Leishmaniasis is a potentially life-threatening parasitic disease caused by an obligate intramacrophagic trypanosomatid of the genus Leishmania (Trypanosomatida: Trypanosomatidae), traditionally trans - mitted by female phlebotomine sand flies (Diptera: Psychodidae) ( Burza et al., 2018 ). Endemic to tropical and subtropical regions worldwide, leishmaniasis is classified as a neglected tropical disease ( WHO, 2023 ). * Corresponding author at: Center of Excellence in Vector Biology and Vector-Borne Disease, Chulalongkorn University, Bangkok 10330, Thailand. Published by Elsevier B.V. This is an open access article under the CC BY license ( http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/ ). Over 50 Leishmania species have been identified, with more than 20 known to infect humans ( Akhoundi et al., 2016 ). Clinically, the disease manifests mainly as cutaneous, mucocutaneous, or visceral forms ( Aronson et al., 2016 ; Mathison and Bradley, 2023 ). Taxonomically, Leishmania parasites are divided into four subgenera: Leishmania , Vian - nia, Sauroleishmania , and the rece

ison and Bradley, 2023 ). Taxonomically, Leishmania parasites are divided into four subgenera: Leishmania , Vian - nia, Sauroleishmania , and the recently established subgenus Mundinia (formerly the Leishmania enriettii complex) ( Espinosa et al., 2018 ). The Mundinia subgenus comprises six Leishmania species: L. enriettii ( Muniz and Medina, 1948 ), L. macropodum ( Rose et al., 2004 ), L. martiniquensis ( Pothirat et al., 2014 ), L. orientalis ( Jariyapan et al., 2018 ), L. chancei , and L. procaviensis ( Kwakye-Nuako et al., 2023 ). Historically, Southeast Asia was considered non-endemic for leish - maniasis, with reported cases mostly imported. However, Thailand has recently been declared endemic by the World Health Organization due to the increasing number of autochthonous cases in the northern and southern regions ( Leelayoova et al., 2017 ; Srivarasat et al., 2022 ; Anu - gulruengkitt et al., 2023 ). Most indigenous cases are attributed to two Leishmania ( Mundinia ) species: L. martiniquensis ( Srivarasat et al., 2022 ) and L. orientalis ( Anugulruengkitt et al., 2023 ). This

ibuted to two Leishmania ( Mundinia ) species: L. martiniquensis ( Srivarasat et al., 2022 ) and L. orientalis ( Anugulruengkitt et al., 2023 ). This rise underscores the urgent need to identify natural vectors for developing effective vector control and transmission interruption strategies, particularly because Leishmania ( Mundinia ) species, which dominate Thai cases, lack confirmed vectors. Unlike the subgenera Leishmania , Viannia , and Sauroleishmania , which are proven to be transmitted by phlebotomine sand flies, vectors for Leishmania ( Mundinia ) remain undetermined despite their growing clinical importance. Experimental studies have shown that several Leishmania ( Mundinia ) species fail to develop in typical sand fly vectors (e.g., Phlebotomus and Lutzomyia ) but reach infective stages in Culicoides sonorensis biting midges (Diptera: Ceratopogonidae) ( Seblova et al., 2015 ; Chanmol et al., 2019 ; Becvar et al., 2021 ). Molecular detection of L. martiniquensis and L. orientalis in Thai field-caught Culicoides near patient residences further suggests biting midges as candi

cular detection of L. martiniquensis and L. orientalis in Thai field-caught Culicoides near patient residences further suggests biting midges as candidate vectors ( Sunantaraporn et al., 2021 ; Songumpai et al., 2022 ; Kaewmee et al., 2023 ; Ampol et al., 2024 ; Promrangsee et al., 2024 ). However, vector confirmation requires fulfilling Killick-Kendrick's criteria, including microscopic parasite development and human blood-feeding, which remain unfulfilled ( Killick-Kendrick, 1999 ). Beyond Leishmania , the molecular detection of diverse trypanosomatids such as Trypanosoma and Crithidia in Thai Culicoides raises intriguing questions about their potential vector competence for this broader parasite group ( Sunantaraporn et al., 2021 ; Songumpai et al., 2022 ; Sunantaraporn et al., 2022 ; Kaewmee et al., 2023 ). To address this evidence gap, we collected Culicoides biting midges from two sites in leishmaniasis foci in Songkhla Province, Southern Thailand: Site 1, previously sampled for sand flies near a visceral leishmaniasis patient's residence in our prior study ( Preativatanyou et

Southern Thailand: Site 1, previously sampled for sand flies near a visceral leishmaniasis patient's residence in our prior study ( Preativatanyou et al., 2023 ), and Site 2, near a new visceral leishmaniasis patient's residence, to characterize trypanosomatid communities in local Culi - coides populations. Accordingly, the specific aims were: (1) microscopic dissection of parous/gravid Culicoides to detect trypanosomatid developmental stages, (2) nanopore metabarcoding to assess trypanosomatid preva - lence and blood meal sources, and (3) integration of these methods to evaluate Culicoides exposure to Leishmania ( Mundinia ) in leishmaniasis foci in Songkhla Province. These findings document naturally occurring trypanosomatids in local Culicoides populations, highlight methodolog - ical synergies and limitations, and provide baseline data for vector incrimination studies. 2. Materials and methods 2.1. Culicoides collection and morphological species identification Culicoides midges were collected over two consecutive nights (6:00 pm-6:00 am) in November 2024 from the house surroundin

gical species identification Culicoides midges were collected over two consecutive nights (6:00 pm-6:00 am) in November 2024 from the house surroundings of two patients with HIV/AIDS previously diagnosed with visceral leishmani - asis caused by L. martiniquensis and residing in Na Thawi District (6 ◦ 39 ′ 27 ″ N, 100 ◦ 42 ′ 48 ″ E; Site 1) and Sadao District (6 ◦ 46 ′ 59 ″ N, 100 ◦ 16 ′ 2 ″ E; Site 2), Songkhla Province, Southern Thailand, respectively ( Fig. 1 ). Both sites used identical trap configurations consisting of Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) light traps equipped with a 25 W ultraviolet light bulb and Mahidol University ultraviolet light-emitting diode (MU UV-LED) light traps. At Site 1, traps were installed 1 – 1.5 m above ground around the household area, and one additional CDC light trap was placed near the chicken coop (~15 m from the house). At Site 2, all traps were placed around the household area (no chicken coop present). The following day, captured insects were immobilized by freezing for 5 – 10 min to facilitate sample handling as previously re

chicken coop present). The following day, captured insects were immobilized by freezing for 5 – 10 min to facilitate sample handling as previously recommended ( Yurkiewicz, 1993 ). The collected insects were then examined under a stereomicroscope (EZ4 HD, Leica, Germany). Female Culicoides samples were separated from other insect species according to specific morphological characteristics, including humeral pits, sensilla coeloconica, claws of similar size, 13 antennal segments, and wing pigmentation patterns ( Wirth and Hubert, 1989 ). In this investigation, nulliparous females, which constituted most of the traps, were excluded from the collection. The presence of a burgundy-red pigment in the abdominal wall was used to differentiate parous fe - males from nulliparous ones ( Dyce, 1969 ). Only parous, gravid, and blood-engorged females that had undergone the gonotrophic cycle were included for downstream analysis. Midge species were identified using the taxonomic keys of Culicoides common in Southeast Asia ( Wirth and Hubert, 1989 ) and the recent morphological description of Culi

identified using the taxonomic keys of Culicoides common in Southeast Asia ( Wirth and Hubert, 1989 ) and the recent morphological description of Culicoides mahasarakhamense ( Pramual et al., 2021 ). 2.2. Microscopic dissection and examination of trypanosomatid development in the digestive tract of Culicoides midges Parous and gravid females were dissected microscopically to inves - tigate the development of Leishmania and other trypanosomatid species in their digestive tract. The dissection was performed on a sterile glass slide under a stereomicroscope. Then, the parasites were examined in the digestive tract using a light microscope (CH20, Olympus, Tokyo, Japan) at 400 × magnification. For the live flagellate sample, the dissected contents were transferred from a slide into a culture flask containing Schneider's insect medium supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum and 25 μ g/mL gentamicin sulfate for parasite isolation. The culture flask was incubated at 26 ◦ C and monitored daily for up to two weeks. If parasites grew in the culture flask, the medium was ali - quoted and smeare

flask was incubated at 26 ◦ C and monitored daily for up to two weeks. If parasites grew in the culture flask, the medium was ali - quoted and smeared onto a glass slide for Giemsa staining to visualize their morphology and identify the parasite species. The remaining parts of the dissected insects were subjected to genomic DNA extraction for further molecular screening of the parasites. 2.3. Genomic DNA extraction Genomic DNA (gDNA) was extracted individually from all Culicoides remains obtained from the previous dissection step and all blood-fed Culicoides samples using the DNeasy Blood & Tissue Kit (Qiagen, Hil - den, Germany) according to the manufacturer's instructions. The con - centration of gDNA was quantified on a Qubit ™ 4 fluorometer using the Qubit ™ dsDNA High Sensitivity Assay Kit (Thermo Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA). The extracted gDNA samples were then kept at 20 ◦ C until further pathogen screening and blood meal analysis. 2.4. Conventional ITS1-PCR and SSU rRNA-PCR Leishmania ITS1-PCR and trypanosomatid SSU rRNA-PCR were per - formed to molecularly screen for Leis

ysis. 2.4. Conventional ITS1-PCR and SSU rRNA-PCR Leishmania ITS1-PCR and trypanosomatid SSU rRNA-PCR were per - formed to molecularly screen for Leishmania spp. and other trypanoso - matids in all extracted gDNA samples, respectively. For Leishmania , primers LITSR2 (5 ′ -CTGGATCATTTTCCGATGATT-3 ′ ) and L5.8Sinner P. Tepboonrueng et al. Infection, Genetics and Evolution 139 (2026) 105912 2 (5 ′ -GTTATGTGAGCCGTTATCC-3 ′ ) amplified the ITS1 region, yielding an approximately 260 – 280 bp product ( Manomat et al., 2017 ). Each 50 μ L PCR reaction contained 25 μ L of 2 × KAPA HiFi HotStart ReadyMix, 1.5 μ L of each 10 μ M primer, 4 μ L of gDNA, and 18 μ L of nuclease-free water. For other trypanosomatids, primers TRY927-F (5 ′ -GAAACAA - GAAACACGGGAG-3 ′ ) and TRY927-R (5 ′ -CTACTGGGCAGCTTGGA-3 ′ ) targeted the SSU rRNA region, producing a 920 – 930 bp amplicon ( Noyes et al., 1999 ). Reaction components were consistent with the ITS1-PCR protocol. For ITS1-PCR, thermal cycling included initial denaturation at 95 ◦ C for 5 min; 40 cycles of 98 ◦ C for 30 s, annealing at 53 ◦ C for 30 s,

rotocol. For ITS1-PCR, thermal cycling included initial denaturation at 95 ◦ C for 5 min; 40 cycles of 98 ◦ C for 30 s, annealing at 53 ◦ C for 30 s, and extension at 72 ◦ C for 30 s; followed by a final extension at 72 ◦ C for 10 min. For SSU rRNA-PCR, thermal cycling consisted of initial dena - turation at 95 ◦ C for 5 min; 40 cycles of 98 ◦ C for 30 s, annealing at 53 ◦ C for 30 s, and extension at 72 ◦ C for 1 min 30 s; followed by a final extension at 72 ◦ C for 10 min. To minimize contamination risk, strict protocols were implemented, including the use of separate rooms for pre-PCR, PCR, and post-PCR steps, dedicated sterile work areas, UV decontamination, and the in - clusion of negative controls. Genomic DNA samples extracted from L. martiniquensis promastigotes (WHO code: MHOM/TH/2012/CULE1) cultivated from the bone marrow of the first patient in this study, and Trypanosoma evansi trypomastigotes, served as positive controls for ITS1-PCR and SSU rRNA-PCR, respectively, while nuclease-free water was used as a negative control. The ITS1 and SSU rRNA amplicon products were veri

r ITS1-PCR and SSU rRNA-PCR, respectively, while nuclease-free water was used as a negative control. The ITS1 and SSU rRNA amplicon products were verified by electrophoresis in 1.5% ( w / v ) agarose gel with ethidium bromide staining and then visualized using the Gel Doc XR + Gel Documentation System (Bio-Rad, Hercules, CA, USA). All positive ITS1/SSU rRNA-PCR products were purified using Agencourt AMPure XP beads (Beckman Coulter, CA, USA) for subsequent library preparation. 2.5. Vertebrate cox1-PCR To identify the blood meal origins of Culicoides midges, gDNA from each blood-engorged midge sample was amplified using conventional PCR targeting a region of the vertebrate-specific cytochrome c oxidase subunit I ( cox 1) gene. The primers VertCOI_7194_F (5 ′ -CGMA - TRAAYAAYATRAGCTTCTGAY-3 ′ ) and Mod_RepCOI_R (5 ′ - TTCDGGRTGNCCRAARAATCA-3 ′ ) were employed to generate an amplicon of approximately 440 bp ( Reeves et al., 2018 ). PCR reactions were prepared in a total volume of 25 μ L, comprising 12.5 μ L of 2 × KAPA HiFi HotStart Ready Mix, 0.75 μ L of each 10 μ M primer, 3 μ L of gD

ctions were prepared in a total volume of 25 μ L, comprising 12.5 μ L of 2 × KAPA HiFi HotStart Ready Mix, 0.75 μ L of each 10 μ M primer, 3 μ L of gDNA, and 8 μ L of nuclease-free water. The thermal cycling protocol consisted of initial denaturation at 95 ◦ C for 5 min, followed by 40 cycles of denaturation at 98 ◦ C for 30 s, annealing at 48.5 ◦ C for 30 s, and extension at 72 ◦ C for 1 min, with a final extension at 72 ◦ C for 10 min. Genomic DNA from a cow was used as a positive control, and nuclease- free water was used as a negative control. All PCR amplicons were individually purified using Agencourt AMPure XP beads (Beckman Coulter, CA, USA). 2.6. MinION ® -based metabarcoding and bioinformatic analysis Purified amplicons of Leishmania ITS1, trypanosomatid SSU rRNA gene, and vertebrate cox 1 gene obtained from previous steps were quantified using the Qubit ™ dsDNA High Sensitivity Assay Kit (Thermo Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA). For each sample, 200 fmol of the target amplicon was end-repaired and ligated with native barcodes supplied in the Native Barcoding Kit 96 V14 (Cat.

For each sample, 200 fmol of the target amplicon was end-repaired and ligated with native barcodes supplied in the Native Barcoding Kit 96 V14 (Cat. No. SQK-NBD114.96, Oxford Nanopore Technologies, Didcot, UK), using the NEBNext Ultra II End Repair/dA-Tailing Module and NEB Blunt/TA Ligase Master Mix (New Fig. 1. m Google Earth ( https://earth.google.com ). P. Tepboonrueng et al. Infection, Genetics and Evolution 139 (2026) 105912 3 England Biolabs, MA, USA), respectively. All barcoded samples were multiplexed and ligated to the sequencing adaptor using the NEBNext Quick Ligation Module (New England Biolabs, MA, USA). After clean- up, 50 fmol of the final ligation product was loaded onto a MinION ® R10.4.1 flow cell for overnight sequencing. Super-accuracy basecalling was performed with Dorado version 7.3.11 using the dna_r10.4.1_e8.2_400bps_sup@v4.3.0 model. Low- quality reads with Q scores below 20 were filtered out using Chopper version 0.7.0 ( De Coster and Rademakers, 2023 ). Additionally, reads were size-selected to exclude those not falling within the anticipated length ranges for each target region: approximately 200 – 350 bases for ITS1, 800 – 1,000 bases for SSU rRNA, and 400 – 500 bases for vertebrate cox 1. Cluster analysis was then performed using amplicon_sorter.py (version 2024_02_20) ( Vierstraete and Braeckman, 2022 ) to generate consensus sequences. To ensure consensus relia

performed using amplicon_sorter.py (version 2024_02_20) ( Vierstraete and Braeckman, 2022 ) to generate consensus sequences. To ensure consensus reliability, a minimum read coverage threshold of 100 reads per cluster was applied, along with an abundance threshold requiring each cluster to represent at least 5% of the total reads per sample. This abundance filter helps eliminate low- frequency clusters likely due to sequencing errors, contamination, or artifacts, while retaining sensitivity for detecting biologically relevant minor sequence variants. Clusters not meeting the coverage or abun - dance criteria were excluded from further analyses. This approach bal - ances minimizing the influence of sequencing errors with maintaining the ability to detect minor species or sequence variants. The resulting sequences were aligned against the GenBank reference using the Basic Local Alignment Search Tool for nucleotides (BLASTn) ( https://blast.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/Blast.cgi ) ( Johnson et al., 2008 ) for taxonomic identification. The number of distinct Leishmania ITS1 and trypanosomatid SSU rRN

.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/Blast.cgi ) ( Johnson et al., 2008 ) for taxonomic identification. The number of distinct Leishmania ITS1 and trypanosomatid SSU rRNA haplotypes was analyzed using DnaSP version 6.12.03 ( http://www.ub.edu/dnasp/ ) ( Rozas et al., 2017 ). The sequences of the unique haplotypes obtained were subsequently sub - mitted to the GenBank database. 2.7. Phylogenetic analyses of Leishmania spp. and other trypanosomatids Leishmania ITS1 haplotypes obtained from the previous consensus analysis in this study, along with reference sequences from GenBank (Supplementary Table S1), were aligned using ClustalW implemented in BioEdit version 7.7.1.0 ( Hall, 1999 ). Phylogenetic relationships were inferred using Bayesian inference (BI) and maximum likelihood (ML) methods. The optimal evolutionary model for the BI analysis was identified using ModelFinder ( Kalyaanamoorthy et al., 2017 ) based on the lowest Bayesian information criterion score, with the K2P + I model selected for our data. A Bayesian phylogenetic tree was generated with BEAST version 2.7.7 ( Bouckaert et al., 2019 ). T

score, with the K2P + I model selected for our data. A Bayesian phylogenetic tree was generated with BEAST version 2.7.7 ( Bouckaert et al., 2019 ). The posterior probability support for each node was calculated from 100,000,000 Markov chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) steps, discarding the first 25% as burn-in. For ML analysis, the MEGA11 software ( Tamura et al., 2021 ) was utilized to determine the best-fitting model (K2P + I) and construct a phylogenetic tree with 1000 bootstrap replicates. For the trypanosomatid SSU rRNA phylogeny, all obtained haplo - types and reference sequences from GenBank (Supplementary Table S2) were aligned using SINA aligner version 1.2.12 ( Pruesse et al., 2012 ), employing reference-based alignment against the curated SILVA SSU rRNA gene database that incorporates secondary structure information to preserve compensatory base changes in paired (stem) regions. The phylogenetic tree was reconstructed using BEAST version 2.7.7 with the TN + I + R3 model, running 100,000,000 MCMC steps with the initial 25% as burn-in. The ML tree was built in MEGA11 with the K2P +

sion 2.7.7 with the TN + I + R3 model, running 100,000,000 MCMC steps with the initial 25% as burn-in. The ML tree was built in MEGA11 with the K2P + G + I model and 1000 bootstrap replicates. Both BI and ML trees, based on Leishmania ITS1 and trypanosomatid SSU rRNA sequences, were visu - alized using FigTree version 1.4.4 ( Rambaut, 2018 ). 3. Results 3.1. Morphological identification and species richness of Culicoides midges A total of 215 parous/gravid and blood-engorged female Culicoides samples (excluding nulliparous) were collected, including 81 parous/ gravid and 20 blood-engorged females from the household of the first patient (Site 1) in Na Thawi District, and 114 parous/gravid females from the area surrounding the second patient's house (Site 2) in Sadao District. Additionally, four male Culicoides were collected: two C. guttifer and one C. huffi from Site 1, and one C. guttifer and one C. orientalis from Site 2. The female midges were morphologically identified as at least ten species across four subgenera, including Meijerehelea , Trithecoides , Avaritia , and Hoffmania

idges were morphologically identified as at least ten species across four subgenera, including Meijerehelea , Trithecoides , Avaritia , and Hoffmania , as well as the Calvipalpis group ( Table 1 ). Culicoides guttifer was the dominant species at both locations, accounting for 66.5% of the total collection, followed by C. ( Trithecoides ) spp., C. huffi , and C. orientalis , which represented 10.7%, 9.8%, and 7.0%, respectively. Additionally, one specimen each of C. peregrinus , C. actoni , and C. jacobsoni was collected near the first patient's household. All blood-engorged specimens were collected exclusively from the trap placed near the chicken coop. 3.2. Direct microscopic observation of live trypanosomatids All 195 non-engorged midge abdomens were dissected under a ste - reomicroscope to examine the presence of live trypanosomatids in their digestive tracts. No visible blood meal remnants were observed in any midguts. No Leishmania promastigotes were observed in any specimens examined; however, heavy colonization by metacyclic trypomastigotes was detected only in the midgut near

promastigotes were observed in any specimens examined; however, heavy colonization by metacyclic trypomastigotes was detected only in the midgut near the stomodeal valve of a single C. guttifer specimen (SJA1) collected from the first patient's household ( Fig. 2 A, Supplementary Video S1). Portions of these heavily colonized midgut contents were used for gDNA extraction, while the remainder was transferred to Schneider's insect medium for parasite cultivation. By day 5, viable and developing metacyclic trypomastigotes were observed and Giemsa-stained, displaying a single kinetoplast at the posterior end, a nucleus, and an undulating membrane with a flagellum extending along the membrane and exiting at the anterior end ( Fig. 2 B – 2 E). Un - fortunately, trypanosome cultivation failed due to contamination — for future studies, it would be beneficial to add an antifungal agent to the medium and combine several types of antibiotics. 3.3. Nanopore metabarcoding and phylogenetic inferences of trypanosomatids in Culicoides midges Leishmania and other trypanosomatid species were molecula

. Nanopore metabarcoding and phylogenetic inferences of trypanosomatids in Culicoides midges Leishmania and other trypanosomatid species were molecularly detected in 35 and 15 of the 215 parous/gravid and blood-engorged female Culicoides samples analyzed, representing overall prevalences of 16.3% and 7.0%, respectively. After ITS1 amplicon sequencing and quality filtering, 432,489 high-quality reads from all positive samples were analyzed using amplicon_sorter.py, resulting in 47 consensus se - quences. Based on BLASTn results, L. martiniquensis was ubiquitously identified in 35 samples across several species, including C. guttifer ( n = 26), C. huffi ( n = 4), C. orientalis ( n = 3), and C. ( Trithecoides ) spp. ( n = 2), showing 99.3 – 100% identity to L. martiniquensis reference sequences (GenBank: JQ001751.1 and MK603826.1 ). Notably, the nucleotide sequence detected in the first patient was previously uploaded to Gen - Bank under the accession number JQ001751.1 ( Chusri et al., 2012 ; Phumee et al., 2014 ). The sequence from the second patient was also 100% identical to that of

he accession number JQ001751.1 ( Chusri et al., 2012 ; Phumee et al., 2014 ). The sequence from the second patient was also 100% identical to that of the first patient, confirming the infection was caused by the same strain in both cases. Additionally, L. orientalis was co- detected with L. martiniquensis in 12 samples, including C. guttifer ( n = 11) and C. ( Trithecoides ) sp. ( n = 1), with 98.9 – 100% identity to L. orientalis references (GenBank: KY982665.1 , KY982671.1 , and OR135533.1 ). P. Tepboonrueng et al. Infection, Genetics and Evolution 139 (2026) 105912 4 Following SSU rRNA amplicon sequencing, 147,290 high-quality reads were analyzed, yielding 16 consensus sequences. Trypanosoma sp. was detected in two C. guttifer samples (SJA1 and SJA40) and one C. huffi specimen (BF02), showing 99.8 – 100% identity to a Trypanosoma sp. reference (GenBank: OP217134.1 ) previously identified in C. guttifer from Phatthalung Province, Southern Thailand, and 98.8 – 99% identity to Trypanosoma bennetti (GenBank: JF778738.1 ), an avian trypanosome infecting the lesser spotted eagle ( Aquil

hern Thailand, and 98.8 – 99% identity to Trypanosoma bennetti (GenBank: JF778738.1 ), an avian trypanosome infecting the lesser spotted eagle ( Aquila pomarina ). In the C. guttifer specimen SJA1, where live metacyclic trypomastigotes were previously observed, molecular analysis confirmed a single Trypanosoma infection, verifying that the live trypomastigotes seen during midgut dissection belong to the genus Trypanosoma and are closely related to T. bennetti . Among monoxenous trypanosomatids, Crithidia sp. was found in two C. guttifer samples (SJA73 and P1_27), exhibiting 99 – 100% identity to Crithidia sp. reference sequences (GenBank: OP698042.1 and OP698046.1 ) previously identified in Culicoides spp. from Songkhla Province, and 95.9 – 96.9% identity to Crithidia thermophila (GenBank: KY264937.1 ). More frequently, Herpetomonas sp. was identified in 11 Culicoides samples, including nine C. guttifer , one C. huffi , and one C. mahasarakhamense , with 91.4 – 91.8% identity to Herpetomonas nabiculae (GenBank: JN624300.2 ), originally reported from the bug Nabis flavomarginatus . et al. Infection, Genetics and Evolution 139 (2026) 105912 6 and Leishmania ( Fig. 3 ). Haplotype sequences from this study clustered within the Mundinia clade in company with reference sequences of L. martiniquensis and L. orientalis previously reported, confirming their classification within this subgenus. Within Mundinia , L. martiniquensis and L. orientalis each formed distinct, well-supported monophyletic subclades. Sequences obtained from Culicoides closely matched known L. martiniquensis and L. orientalis isolates from human cases in Thailand, including those from patients in this study. This genetic similarity suggests that Culicoides were exposed to the same Leishmania strains infecting local human cases. Phylogenetic analyses of other trypanosomatids using SSU rRNA sequences yielded consistent, well-supported results, dividing Trypa - nosomatidae into distinct clades, including Crithidia , Herpetomonas , Trypanosoma , and related genera ( Fig. 4 ). The Crithidia clade included many sequences from Culicoides midges — both previous and current studies — grouping closely with

ted genera ( Fig. 4 ). The Crithidia clade included many sequences from Culicoides midges — both previous and current studies — grouping closely with known Crithidia species. This confirms their placement in the genus and highlights extensive genetic diversity among Thai midge hosts. The Herpetomonas clade formed a robust, distinct lineage with several known species and novel sequences from Culicoides samples in this study. Despite modest BLASTn matches (91.4 – 91.8% identity) to H. nabiculae , both analyses firmly positioned these sequences within Herpetomonas , alongside basal species like H. mirabilis and H. wanderleyi . These results verify the presence of diverse monoxenous trypanosomatids in local midges. The Trypanosoma clade stood out as a distinct lineage, spanning vertebrate-infecting species and new Culicoides -derived sequences from this work. Two haplotypes closely matched Trypanosoma sp. (GenBank: OP217134.1 ) from C. guttifer in Southern Thailand, aligning in the same subclade as avian T. bennetti (GenBank: JF778738.1 ). This points to avian-associated Trypanosoma in b

ifer in Southern Thailand, aligning in the same subclade as avian T. bennetti (GenBank: JF778738.1 ). This points to avian-associated Trypanosoma in biting midges and a broader host range in Thailand. The associations between Culicoides species and trypano - somatids are illustrated in Fig. 5 . 3.4. These trimmed sequences were taxonomically assigned using BLASTn analysis (Sup - plementary Table S3). ondrial cox 1 reference (GenBank: KT587576.1 ). 4. Discussion Phlebotomine sand flies are widely recognized as primary vectors of leishmaniasis ( Cecílio et al., 2022 ). Nonetheless, recent experimental and molecular evidence suggests that several Leishmania species within the subgenus Mundinia , including L. martiniquensis and L. orientalis , may be transmitted by Culicoides midges rather than sand flies ( Chanmol et al., 2019 ; Becvar et al., 2021 ; Sunantaraporn et al., 2021 ; Songumpai et al., 2022 ; Kaewmee et al., 2023 ; Ampol et al., 2024 ; Promrangsee et al., 2024 ). Various Culicoides species have been proposed as probable vectors: C. mahasarakhamense , C. orientalis , C. innoxius , and C. oxystoma in Northern Thailand ( Sunantaraporn et al., 2021 ; Ampol et al., 2024 ; Promrangsee et al., 2024 ), and C. peregrinus and C. oxystoma in Southern Thailand ( Songumpai et al., 2022 ; Kaewmee et al., 2023 ). However, definitive incrimination requires demonstration of metacyclic develop - ment in the vector and human blood feeding, as outlined by Killick- Kendrick's criteria ( Killi

ination requires demonstration of metacyclic develop - ment in the vector and human blood feeding, as outlined by Killick- Kendrick's criteria ( Killick-Kendrick, 1999 ). Despite targeting the chicken coop at Site 1 — a prime peri-domestic sand fly habitat — our traps captured minimal phlebotomine sand flies ( < 5 individuals total) at both sites, while Culicoides dominated the collections. This contrasts with our previous survey at Site 1, six years earlier (during the rainy season), where higher sand fly numbers enabled the detection of Leishmania DNA in Grassomyia indica and Phlebotomus stantoni ( Preativatanyou et al., 2023 ). The low phlebotomine captures likely result from: (1) dry season timing (November 2024); (2) higher trap heights (1 – 1.5 m), less favorable for ground-dwelling phlebotomines; and (3) temporal population vari - ability driven by microclimate (temperature, humidity, rainfall, wind velocity) and host availability ( Salom ´on et al., 2004 ; Alten et al., 2015 ; Buatong et al., 2022 ). Culicoides collections from two sites in this work were similarly predominat

y ( Salom ´on et al., 2004 ; Alten et al., 2015 ; Buatong et al., 2022 ). Culicoides collections from two sites in this work were similarly predominated by C. guttifer , a widespread species across diverse habitats in Thailand ( Gomontean et al., 2023 ). Blood-fed individuals of C. guttifer , C. huffi , and C. mahasarakhamense were mainly trapped near a chicken coop in the first patient's household, matching their ornitho - philic preferences ( Jomkumsing et al., 2021 ; Sunantaraporn et al., 2022 ; Promrangsee et al., 2024 ; Gomontean et al., 2023 ; Kyi Soe et al., 2025 ). Trap-specific variations in species composition and abundance of Culi - coides midges resulted from habitat differences, microclimate, season - ality, host proximity, trap placement, and animal sheds ( Ayll ´on et al., 2014 ; Collins et al., 2018 ; Cu ´ellar et al., 2018 ; England et al., 2020 ; Thepparat et al., 2024 ). This study aimed to dissect parous and gravid female Culicoides to detect Leishmania promastigote development microscopically, as well as estimate trypanosomatid prevalence and blood meal sources m

emale Culicoides to detect Leishmania promastigote development microscopically, as well as estimate trypanosomatid prevalence and blood meal sources molecu - larly. Nulliparous females were excluded from dissections and molecular screening because we prioritized parous/gravid females, which had completed gonotrophic cycles and had the highest potential for dixenous parasite development. However, nulliparous females would have pro - vided useful control data, as they are expected to harbor only mon - oxenous trypanosomatids without dixenous parasites, enabling the quantification of baseline monoxenous prevalence. generates a single chromatogram repre - senting one parasite species or host ( Ohta et al., 2023 ; Preativatanyou et al., 2024 ). Notably, the single C. guttifer specimen (SJA1) exhibited dense colonization by viable metacyclic trypomastigotes, which were identi - fied molecularly as being closely related to the avian trypanosome T. bennetti (or potentially T. bennetti itself). Deep SSU rRNA amplicon sequencing of this specimen confirmed Trypanosoma as the sole trypa - nosomatid genus present, and no co-infections with Leishmania or other trypanosomatids were detected despite their prevalence in other speci - mens. This molecular validation eliminates concerns about culture bias, where only the highest-density trypanosomatid will successfully grow while low-density co-infections in the insect gut remain undetected and unconsidered. Experimental infections have previously demonstrated that T. everetti can establish mature infections in the midguts of Culi - coides ( Monoculicoides ) nubeculosus and Culicoides ( Culicoides ) impunc - tatus , while T. bennetti and Trypanosoma avium can

he midguts of Culi - coides ( Monoculicoides ) nubeculosus and Culicoides ( Culicoides ) impunc - tatus , while T. bennetti and Trypanosoma avium can develop in C. nubeculosus , supporting vector competence of Culicoides for avian trypanosomes ( Svobodov ´a et al., 2017 ; Bernotien ˙e et al., 2020 ; Chagas et al., 2025 ). Therefore, our finding represents the first natural evidence of mature avian trypanosome infection in Thai Culicoides and is sup - ported by metabarcoding data indicating chickens as the main verte - brate host. In contrast, nanopore metabarcoding detected Leishmania ( Mundinia ) DNA, including L. martiniquensis , L. orientalis , and multiple haplotypes, in Culicoides specimens. However, no promastigote stages were observed microscopically, despite transparent guts amenable to trypanosomatid detection. This discrepancy between molecular prevalence and micro - scopic negativity reflects several factors: (1) microscopy detection threshold, with only high-density infections visible while low-density infections remain undetected; (2) delayed trap retrieval causing spec

etection threshold, with only high-density infections visible while low-density infections remain undetected; (2) delayed trap retrieval causing spec - imen desiccation, leading to collapsed and shriveled internal structures that obscure clear visualization; and (3) tissue fragility, particularly of the cardia region near the stomodeal valve, where mature Leishmania ( Mundinia ) infections are typically localized, while the rest of the gut remains empty ( Seblova et al., 2015 ; Becvar et al., 2021 ); and (4) developmental timing, as mature infections form a plug consisting of haptomonads, leptomonads, and metacyclic forms reinforced with pro - mastigote secretory gel, filling and expanding the cardia region - — making high-density late-stage infections more visible, whereas early infections lack sufficient time post-bloodmeal for such development ( Seblova et al., 2015 ; Becvar et al., 2021 ). Genetics and Evolution 139 (2026) 105912 9 DNA, even when containing chicken blood. Previous studies indicate that Leishmania cannot multiply in chickens due to their elevated body temperature (~41 ◦ C) ( Zilberstein and Shapira, 1994 ; Alexander et al., 2002 ), despite eliciting antibody responses ( Tatto et al., 2023 ). No viable infections have been documented in chickens ( Alexander et al., 2002 ; Otranto et al., 2010 ). Additionally, 91% of the female collection were parous/gravid, indicating multiple gonotrophic cycles with po - tential for cumulative blood source diversity across feeding history. In light of feeding plasticity and multi-cycle history of Culicoides , and inability of Leishmania to infect chickens, several parous/gravid and chicken blood-engorged specimens tested positive for mammalian Leishmania DNA despite negative microscopy, raising three possible explanations: (1) non-viable residual DNA from previous mammalian blood meals, especially cumulative exposures in multiple-fed females rather than active infection or host switching during current meals; (2) viable

n blood meals, especially cumulative exposures in multiple-fed females rather than active infection or host switching during current meals; (2) viable submicroscopic infections with very low numbers of living par - asites undetectable by light microscopy; and (3) laboratory contami - nation (excluded by strict protocols and negative controls in this work). Positive detections thus reflect (1) and/or (2), as demonstrated by Seblova et al. (2012) , who detected Leishmania infantum DNA in the guts of C. nubeculosus up to 7 days post-bloodmeal despite microscopically negative findings. According to Killick-Kendrick's criteria ( Killick-Kendrick, 1999 ), these findings provide evidence of Leishmania DNA in field-collected Culicoides from endemic areas, opportunistic human blood-feeding, and ecological overlap with clinical cases, suggesting potential host- vector contact. Nonetheless, metabarcoding reveals the presence of target DNA but cannot differentiate between transient remnants and viable parasites, or confirm infectivity or vector competence ( Peters et al., 2004 ; Huggins et al.,

ot differentiate between transient remnants and viable parasites, or confirm infectivity or vector competence ( Peters et al., 2004 ; Huggins et al., 2019 ; Chagas et al., 2025 ). Therefore, PCR- positive Culicoides signify exposed individuals through blood-feeding, not vector competence. Confirming actual infections and trans - missibility requires proof through experimental trials, direct observation of viable metacyclic promastigotes, and confirmation of parasite development ( Dost ´alov ´a and Volf, 2012 ; Becvar et al., 2021 ). Similarly, these data neither implicate chickens as reservoirs nor suggest that the feeding flexibility of C. guttifer drives transmission. The co-detection of L. martiniquensis and L. orientalis , predominantly in C. guttifer , suggests sympatric circulation that can be acquired by the same midge populations, aligning with a previous report of their co- circulation in Thai Culicoides ( Songumpai et al., 2022 ). Additionally, L. orientalis exhibited greater haplotype diversity, consistent with its established microheterogeneity in Culicoides and asymptoma

2 ). Additionally, L. orientalis exhibited greater haplotype diversity, consistent with its established microheterogeneity in Culicoides and asymptomatic Thai humans ( Ruang-Areerate et al., 2023 ; Ampol et al., 2024 ; Promrangsee et al., 2024 ). In this study, specimens were collected near the households of two patients in Songkhla Province: Case 1 (Na Thawi District; treated 10 years ago, non-active) ( Phumee et al., 2014 ) and Case 2 (Sadao District; ongoing since 2022 due to irregular HIV therapy). Notably, Leishmania DNA was detected in Culicoides from both sites despite Case 1 being a non-active case, suggesting the presence of asymptomatic humans or animal reservoirs that sustain local transmission at both locations. Importantly, the predominant L. martiniquensis haplotype in midges matched the parasite haplotype identified from both patients, confirm - ing circulation of the human-pathogenic strain. The detection of Leish - mania DNA in midges from both sites thus likely reflects acquisition from these cryptic local sources rather than direct epidemiological links to the iden

a DNA in midges from both sites thus likely reflects acquisition from these cryptic local sources rather than direct epidemiological links to the identified patients. This interpretation aligns with accumulating evi - dence of Leishmania reservoirs across Southern Thailand. This is sup - ported by recent molecular and serological evidence of Leishmania infection in 19% of asymptomatic blood donors from Trang Province, Southern Thailand ( Piyaraj et al., 2024 ), along with prior findings of anti- Leishmania antibodies in dogs and cats from endemic areas ( Junsiri et al., 2017 ; Sriwongpan et al., 2021 ), as well as the detection of L. martiniquensis DNA in black rats ( Chusri et al., 2014 ; Sriwongpan et al., 2021 ). In addition to Leishmania and avian trypanosomes, monoxenous trypanosomatids Herpetomonas and Crithidia were molecularly detected, marking the first report of Herpetomonas in Thai Culicoides , predomi - nantly C. guttifer . Previously recorded species, such as Herpetomonas ztiplika from the hindgut of Culicoides ( Oecacta ) kibunensis based on microscopic evidence ( Podli

Previously recorded species, such as Herpetomonas ztiplika from the hindgut of Culicoides ( Oecacta ) kibunensis based on microscopic evidence ( Podlipaev et al., 2004 ) — and more recently detected molecularly in Culicoides ( Avaritia ) obsoletus ( Ho ˇskov ´a et al., 2025 ) — and Herpetomonas trimorpha from the Malpighian tubules of Culicoides ( Oecacta ) truncarum ( Zídkov ´a et al., 2010 ), highlight their occurrence in ceratopogonids. Our Herpetomonas sequences exhibited 8.2 – 8.6% divergence from H. nabiculae and formed a well-supported monophyletic clade, suggesting a novel species. Infection, Genetics and Evolution 139 (2026) 105912 10 and L. orientalis in one C. guttifer (SJA49), demonstrating the capability of nanopore metabarcoding to detect co-occurring trypanosomatids in field-collected specimens. These represent typical insect commensals in Diptera ( Maslov et al., 2013 ; Luke ˇs et al., 2018 ; Frolov et al., 2021 ), with rare associations in immunocompromised humans ( Morio et al., 2008 ; Boucinha et al., 2022 ), but no clinical reports have been documented in Thailand. Together, these findings comprehensively demonstrate the capa - bility of nanopore metabarcoding to detect multi-parasite communities, resolve haplotype diversity, and identify blood meal sources, including opportunistic human feeding, in field-collected Culicoides . As discussed earlier, this methodology cannot establish vector competence for Leishmania , requiring explicit experimental demonstration of parasite development, metacyclic dif

methodology cannot establish vector competence for Leishmania , requiring explicit experimental demonstration of parasite development, metacyclic differentiation, and transmissibility potential. Complementary microscopy remains essential for distinguishing viable Leishmania infections from non-viable residual DNA. These findings generate testable hypotheses regarding peri-domestic reservoirs poten - tially sustaining Leishmania ( Mundinia ) circulation. In contrast, the natural T. bennetti -related infection documented in C. guttifer provides definitive field evidence affirming their established vector competence for avian trypanosomes. Ultimately, clarifying vectorial capacity of Culicoides for leishmaniasis transmission requires future integrated studies combining molecular surveillance with controlled experimental infections, detailed microscopic examinations, inclusion of all gono - trophic stages for comprehensive parasite profiling, reservoir surveys, and targeted screening of asymptomatic human populations. 5. ng, reservoir surveys, and targeted screening of asymptomatic human populations. 5. Conclusions This study provides the first comprehensive characterization of the trypanosomatid community in Culicoides biting midges from a leishmaniasis-endemic region of Southern Thailand, integrating micro - scopy with nanopore metabarcoding. Nanopore metabarcoding enabled high-resolution detection of multi-parasite communities, haplotypes, and blood meals. Although Leishmania ( Mundinia ) DNA was widely detected, no promastigotes were observed microscopically, demon - strating the critical limitation of molecular detection in distinguishing viable parasites from non-viable residual DNA from previous mamma - lian blood meals, especially in multi-fed females, or submicroscopic infections. Local Culicoides represent candidate but not confirmed vec - tors of leishmaniasis, as shown by field exposure to parasite DNA, ecological overlap with cases, and opportunistic human feeding; how - ever, there is no definitive evidence of parasite development, trans - missibility, or established epidemiological lin

ortunistic human feeding; how - ever, there is no definitive evidence of parasite development, trans - missibility, or established epidemiological links required for confirmed vector status. Conversely, live T. bennetti -related trypomastigotes in C. guttifer confirm, for the first time, natural avian trypanosome infec - tion in Thai Culicoides , establishing vector competence for an avian parasite. These complementary approaches establish baseline data on trypanosomatid diversity and feeding patterns, serving as an essential foundation for future vector competence studies.
